docs.title: Central_Bank_Policies.Insurance_related_Central_Bank_Deposits
docs.text:
stock

Units: b$ · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_insurance_payout, Central_Bank_Policies.deposit_earnings

It is the stock that records the cumulative scale of central bank insurance intervention over time. This “deposit” does not mean idle savings or a literal bank account balance; it represents how much the central bank has already injected through the insurance channel. The stock increases through insurance payout and safe interest earnings, so it accumulates both direct intervention and the interest earned on that accumulated position. This variable is included because the model needs to track the size and persistence of the central bank’s insurance-related balance-sheet exposure. The initial value is zero (no intervention).

Initial value: 

Central_Bank_Policies.initial_Bank_owned_Central_Bank_Deposit

docs.title: Central_Bank_Policies.Investment_related_Central_Bank_Deposits
docs.text:
stock

Units: b$ · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_investment_financing, Central_Bank_Policies.investment_related_deposit_earning · Outflows: Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_interest_payment

In central bank insurance, the variable called “central bank deposit” is NOT a pile of money sitting somewhere. It does not mean savings, reserves, or a bank account money collected earlier. Instead, in FRIDA it means “How big the central bank’s intervention has been so far.”

Initial value: 

Central_Bank_Policies."initial_Firm-owned_Central_Bank_Deposit"

docs.title: Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_Public_Investments_fraction
docs.text:
stock

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Central_Bank_Policies.increase_in_CB_public_investment_fraction · Outflows: Central_Bank_Policies.decrease_in_CB_public_investment_fraction

This stock is the policy-controlled share of nominal GDP that the CB invests each year.

Initial value: 

Central_Bank_Policies.initial_Central_Bank_Investments_Policy

docs.title: Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_insurance_payout
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the annual flow of funds the central bank provides to cover a share of climate-caused risky defaults. It exists because the model treats central bank insurance as an explicit policy mechanism that absorbs part of the climate-default burden which would otherwise remain in the private financial system.

Equation: 

Finance.total_climate_caused_risky_defaults * Government_Regulations.fraction_of_default_covered_by_central_bank_insurance

docs.title: Central_Bank_Policies.deposit_earnings
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the annual return earned on the accumulated stock of insurance-related central bank deposits. It is included because once the central bank has built up this intervention position, the model assumes that position earns the safe interest rate rather than remaining inert. The equation is Central bank deposit × safe interest, so earnings increase when either the accumulated intervention stock becomes larger or the safe interest rate becomes higher. This variable matters because it gives the central bank insurance stock some endogenous financial dynamics and prevents it from behaving like a purely one-way accounting accumulation.

Equation: 

Central_Bank_Policies.Insurance_related_Central_Bank_Deposits*Government.safe_interest

docs.title: Central_Bank_Policies.increase_in_CB_public_investment_fraction
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

For example, if “CB investments policy stance” is equal to 0,01, “CB investments policy” should increase by about 0,0025 after one year.

If real GDP growth last year was below the target, the policy should become more supportive this year.

Equation: 

IF Central_Bank_Policies.CB_investments_policy_stance >=0
THEN Central_Bank_Policies.CB_investments_policy_stance / Central_Bank_Policies.increase_denominator_CB_investment_policy
ELSE 0

docs.title: Central_Bank_Policies.decrease_in_CB_public_investment_fraction
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

For example, if “CB investments policy stance” is equal to -0,01, “CB investments policy” should decrease by about 0,00125 after one year.

If growth was above target, the policy can back off.

Equation: 

IF Central_Bank_Policies.CB_investments_policy_stance < 0 AND Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_Public_Investments_fraction > 0
THEN -Central_Bank_Policies.CB_investments_policy_stance / Central_Bank_Policies.decrease_denominator_CB_investment_policy
ELSE 0

If the economy was weak last year (negative real GDP growth), the CB increases its investment stance. If the economy was strong last year (positive real GDP growth), the CB reduces its investment stance.

Smoothing horizon for real GDP growth used in the CB investment stance. This is a controller-smoothing parameter, not a policy target. Keep it long enough to filter cycle noise; 10–25 years is a defensible trend-growth window for a structural model.

20

docs.title: Circular_Flow.owner_consumption
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

Forced climate-related relocation spending is treated as owner consumption expenditure. Even though it is not voluntary consumption (like food, leisure, electronics), it is still a HHs expense that reduces their savings and affects their ability to consume other goods. Additionally, Owner households represent the wealthy asset-owning segment.

Equation: 

Circular_Flow.actual_owner_consumption+Circular_Flow.Actual_people_retreat_cost_from_SLR

{MAX(smoothed_owner_net_income*Circular_Flow.minimum_fraction_of_income_to_consumption;
(smoothed_owner_net_income*Circular_Flow.fraction_of_owner_income_to_consumption)*(1+Inflation.inflation)-Circular_Flow.annual_owner_savings_to_reach_goal)

{MAX (adjusted_owner_consumption; Circular_Flow.net_owner_income*minimum_fraction_of_owner_income_to_consumption)

docs.title: Circular_Flow.firms_paying_interest
docs.text:
flow

Units: B$/year · Dimensions: Run

Firms have borrowed money from banks (bank assets = firm liabilities).

Equation: 

(Finance.risky_bank_assets*Finance.risky_interest)
+ (Finance.private_safe_bank_assets*Finance.average_private_debt_safe_interest)
+(Finance.exploratory_bank_assets*Finance.exploratory_interest)
+ Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_interest_payment

docs.title: Circular_Flow.worker_savings_interest
docs.text:
flow

Units: B$/year · Dimensions: Run

Workers earn interest income on their savings. Households deposit their savings in banks. Banks pay them a safe interest rate on these deposits.

The worker savings earn interest equal to the average safe private debt interest rate, because: - Banks pay a lower, stable interest on deposits. - Deposits are considered safe liabilities. - A smoother, slow-moving rate prevents unrealistic volatility in household income.

Thus, worker interest income reflects a delayed and damped response to monetary policy.

Equation: 

Circular_Flow.Worker_Savings*Finance.average_private_debt_safe_interest

docs.title: Circular_Flow.transfers
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

Transfers represent public payments to households (workers) that support income: unemployment benefits, retirement support, child support, etc. In macroeconomics, transfers are a redistribution from government to households but not payment for production.

Equation: 

Government.government_transfers

docs.title: Circular_Flow.insurance_taxation
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

Insurance taxation takes a small percentage of owner wealth each year (after the policy starts) to fund government-backed climate insurance, reducing financial instability. This means the tax is levied as a percentage of owner wealth after the policy starts, and is zero before then. The model includes it because owners hold most wealth and much of the exposed asset base, so they are treated as the main contributors to the insurance scheme.

Equation: 

IF Government_Regulations.fraction_of_default_covered_by_government_insurance > 0
THEN Government.government_insurance_taxation_rate * Circular_Flow.owner_savings
ELSE 0

docs.title: Circular_Flow.desired_worker_savings
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$ · Dimensions: Run

It is the target level of savings that workers want to have, based on their income.

Workers want to hold savings proportional to their income. They adjust their actual savings toward this target gradually, not instantly. This reflects real-world economic behavior where households have desired saving goals tied to income and adjust slowly.

Equation: 

{SMTH1((Circular_Flow.net_worker_income*Circular_Flow.desired_savings_as_multiple_of_wages); Circular_Flow.time_to_consider_desired_savings)}

SMTH1((Circular_Flow.net_worker_income*Circular_Flow.desired_savings_as_multiple_of_wages), Circular_Flow.time_to_consider_desired_savings)

docs.title: Circular_Flow.actual_worker_consumption
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

Workers do not instantly consume the amount they indicate they want to. They adjust gradually over time (over roughly 1.46 years after calibration).

It is multiplied by (1 + inflation effect on consumption) because inflation influence how much they actually consume. Inflation here acts like a behavioral modifier rather than a pure addition or subtraction.

SMTH1(Circular_Flow.indicated_worker_consumption, Circular_Flow.consumption_habits_adjustment_time)*(1+Circular_Flow.inflation_effect_on_consumption)

docs.text:

This is the raw consumption desire before the smoothing that creates actual worker consumption. This equation defines how much workers want to consume given their income, their minimum required consumption, and their savings goal.

net_worker_income*fraction_of_income_to_consumption-annual_worker_savings_to_reach_goal --> How much workers normally want to spend minus how much they need to save each year to catch up to their savings goal.

Equation: 

MAX(Circular_Flow.net_worker_income*Circular_Flow.minimum_fraction_of_income_to_consumption,
Circular_Flow.net_worker_income*Circular_Flow.fraction_of_income_to_consumption-Circular_Flow.annual_worker_savings_to_reach_goal)

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

If the government turns on the wage-tax policy, then slowly adjust the wage tax rate from its old level to the new policy level over a chosen number of years. If the policy is not turned on, keep the old tax rate.

Equation: 

IF TIME > Government_Regulations.wage_tax_policy_start_year AND Government_Regulations.switch_wage_tax_policy = 1 
THEN Circular_Flow.wage_tax_rate[Run] + RAMP((Government_Regulations.wage_tax_policy-Circular_Flow.wage_tax_rate[Run])/Government_Regulations.time_to_implement_wage_tax_policy, Government_Regulations.wage_tax_policy_start_year, Government_Regulations.wage_tax_policy_start_year+ Government_Regulations.time_to_implement_wage_tax_policy) 
ELSE Circular_Flow.wage_tax_rate[Run]

docs.title: Coastal_Assets.Coastal_assets

stock

Units: bc$ · Dimensions: Run, Coastal_Protection_level · Inflows: Coastal_Assets.Increase_of_coastal_asset_values_through_internal_growth · Outflows: Coastal_Assets.Proactive_retreat_of_coastal_assets, Coastal_Assets.Reactive_retreat_of_coastal_assets, Coastal_Assets.Loss_of_coastal_assets_that_are_not_rebuild_after_storm_surge

Initial value: 

Coastal_Assets.Initial_split_of_asset_distribution[Coastal_Protection_level] * INIT(Coastal_Assets.Net_global_financial_assets_in_real_terms[Run]) * Coastal_Assets.Initial_fraction_of_assets_that_are_on_the_coast[Run]

docs.title: Coastal_Assets.Increase_of_coastal_asset_values_through_internal_growth
docs.text:
flow

Units: bc$/year · Dimensions: Run, Coastal_Protection_level

Equation: 

IF Coastal_Protection_level = Coastal_Protection_level.well_protected THEN
Coastal_Assets.Actual_coastal_asset_growth[Run,well_protected]+Coastal_Assets.Investment_going_from_insufficiently_to_well_protected_coastal_assets[Run]
ELSE IF Coastal_Protection_level = Coastal_Protection_level.insufficient THEN
Coastal_Assets.Actual_coastal_asset_growth[Run,insufficient]
ELSE
NAN

docs.title: Coastal_Assets.Proactive_retreat_of_coastal_assets
docs.text:
flow

Units: bc$/year · Dimensions: Run, Coastal_Protection_level

Immobile assets can only be abandoned by people who still had some assets before they left. Assume that these are only the people which are retreating proactively.

Proactive retreat is in this model actually only happening after a retreat time scale. However, we immediately deduct this from the coastal assets stock, to avoid double counting of damages. The value of these assets that is lost is then assumed to be fully part of retreat damage, so that basically decommissioned assets are safe from flood damages, which is kind of wrong.

Equation: 

Coastal_Assets.Coastal_assets* Coastal_Assets.relative_annual_proactive_retreat_of_assets / (1.0 - Coastal_Assets.Total_removed_fraction_of_original_asset_distribution )

Coastal_Assets.Theoretical_coastal_asset_value_growth *  Coastal_Assets.Likelihood_that_people_still_invest_in_coastal_zones
ELSE
Coastal_Assets.Theoretical_coastal_asset_value_growth

docs.text:
aux

Units: 1 · Dimensions: Run, Coastal_Protection_level

This function was fitted to DIVA data for different levels of effective flood height.

Equation: 

(Coastal_Assets.Asset_storm_susceptibility_max_fraction[Coastal_Protection_level] / (1.0 + EXP(-Coastal_Assets.Asset_storm_susceptibility_logistic_growth_rate[Coastal_Protection_level] * (Sea_level_rise_adaptation.Net_global_flood_height - Coastal_Assets.Asset_storm_susceptibility_halfpoint[Coastal_Protection_level]) ) )) + Coastal_Assets.Asset_storm_susceptibility_baseline_value[Coastal_Protection_level]

docs.title: Coastal_Assets.Actually_susceptible_coastal_asset_fraction
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1 · Dimensions: Run, Coastal_Protection_level

Assume here that the asset fraction that is susceptible to storm damages is always larger than the fraction of inundated or retreated assets, Also, assume that inundated or retreated assets where part of those assets that were susceptible to storm damages.

Equation: 

MAX(0, (Coastal_Assets.Fraction_of_original_asset_distribution_that_is_generally_susceptible_to_storm_surge_damage-Coastal_Assets.Total_removed_fraction_of_original_asset_distribution)/(1.0 - Coastal_Assets.Total_removed_fraction_of_original_asset_distribution))

docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: 1 · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.75 – 1

This is 0 in CIAM, assuming that everything is rebuilt. Here, we assume that 0 - 20% are not rebuilt after a storm surge.

Equation: 

0.9

docs.title: Coastal_Assets.Initial_fraction_of_assets_that_are_on_the_coast
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: 1 · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.12 – 0.14

This is the 2010 value, but in the future we should account for coastal migration to get this right.

The value comes from taking population data for the segments in DIVA and multiply them with the regional GDP per capita values to get coastal GDP. This is then divided by the total global GDP.

Equation: 

0.13

docs.title: Coastal_population.Number_of_people_flooded_annually_in_storm_surges
docs.text:
aux

Units: Mp/year · Dimensions: Run, Coastal_Protection_level

If there is an improved distinction between protected/unprotected coastline, this should probably also be reworked.

Numbers from McMichael et al. (2020) ! WATCH OUT: Some number are for 1/100-year flood plains, other for 1/1000-year flood plains!

Hoozemans et al, 1993 (Book): - 200-250 Mio. people live in 1/1000-year flood plain in 1990 - number increases by 50% for SLR of 1m, assuming no other change

Nicholls (2002): - 10 Mio people flooded annually in 1990 - In 2100 this could be 83-510 Mio people annually (without flood protection)

Nicholls (2004): - in 2080, 2-50 Mio people are additionally affected by flooding annually, depending on scenario --> Could check SLR in each scenario to create a number of people/metre...

Nicholls and Tol (2006): -> they also assume 200 Mio people living in the 1/1000-year coastal flood plain in 1990

Pardeans et al (2011): -> Without upgrading defences 84 Mio additional people are flooded annually in business-as-usual scenario

Vafeides et al. (2011): - In 2000: 628 Mio people live in 1/100-year flood plain (?, Maybe this is rather the number for people in "low elevation coastal zones", which often means coastal areas below 10 m above sea level) - future increase is mostly because of migration towards the coast and less because of SLR

Jongman et al (2012): - in 2010: 271 Mio people live in 1/100-year flood plain - in 2050: 345 Mio people live in 1/100-year flood plain --> This might not consider climate change, i.e. SLR ?!

Hinkel et al. (2014); - in 2100: 0.2-4.6% of world population will be flooded annually under 25-123 cm of GMSLR - in 2100: 93-310 Mio people will live in the 1/100-year flood plain

Neumann et al. (2015): - In 2000: 189 Mio people in 1/100-year flood plain - In 2030: 282-285 Mio people ... - In 2060: 315-411 Mio people

  • In 2030: 879-949 Mio people in LECZ (10.8-11.3%)
  • In 2060: 1053-1388 Mio people in LECZ (11.8 - 13.3 %)

Muis et al. (2016): - In 2000: 76 Mio people live in 1/100-year flood plain

Reimann et al. (2023): - In 2000: 634 Mio people (10.3% of total population) live in LECZ, 360 Mio of this are urban areas (56.7%) - In 2015: 815 Mio people (11.0%), 487 Mio urban (59.7%) - In 2020: 898 Mio people (11.4%)

Equation: 

(1.0 - Coastal_Assets.Flood_damage_resilience)*Coastal_population.People_that_experience_storm_surges_because_of_SLR

docs.title: Coastal_population.Fatality_rate_in_flood_events
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: 1 · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.005 – 0.02

0.01 is as in CIAM (Diaz, 2016, SI), based on Jonkman and Vrijling (2008)

Diaz, D. B. (2016). Estimating global damages from sea level rise with the Coastal Impact and Adaptation Model (CIAM). Climatic Change, 137(1–2), 143–156. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-016-1675-4

Jonkman S, Vrijling J (2008) Loss of life due to floods. Journal of Flood Risk Management 1(1):43– 56

Equation: 

[1] = 0.01

ELSE Energy_Balance_Model.Surface_Temperature_Anomaly*Coastal_sea_level_rise.Sensitivity_of_expected_SLR_to_T_anomaly + Emissions.Total_CO2_Emissions* Coastal_sea_level_rise.Sensitivity_of_expected_SLR_to_CO2_emissions

docs.title: Employment.effect_of_climate_on_high_exposure_labour
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

DOI: 10.1016/S2542-5196(21)00170-4

Equation: 

IF (Climate.switch_labor_damage =0) AND TIME > Climate.climate_impact_stoptime THEN
Employment.previous_effect_of_climate_on_high_exposure_labour
ELSE
1 + Employment.UNIT_percent*(Employment.Temperature_effect_on_high_exposure_productivity*(Energy_Balance_Model.Surface_Temperature_Anomaly - Employment.Surface_Temperature_1986_to_2005_offset_relative_to_1850_to_1900) + Employment.Temperature_squared_effect_on_high_exposure_productivity*(Energy_Balance_Model.Surface_Temperature_Anomaly - Employment.Surface_Temperature_1986_to_2005_offset_relative_to_1850_to_1900)^2)

aux Climate-impact parameter

Units: MLabour · Dimensions: Run

Equation: 

Employment.Raw_Labour_Supply_agriculture*
(
Employment.labour_exposure_shares_in_agriculture[No_Exposure]*
1
+
Employment.labour_exposure_shares_in_agriculture[Low_Exposure]*
Employment.effect_of_climate_on_low_exposure_labour
+
Employment.labour_exposure_shares_in_agriculture[High_Exposure]*

)

stock

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Finance.change_in_risk_premium

Risk premium is the extra interest rate banks charge on risky loans on top of a safe benchmark rate to compensate for expected defaults and uncertainty.

stock

Initial value: 

(Finance.Initial_safe_assets-Government.initial_government_debt)
- (Finance.Initial_safe_assets-Government.initial_government_debt) * Finance.initial_fraction_of_safe_loans_financing_innovation
- (Finance.Initial_safe_assets-Government.initial_government_debt) * Finance.initial_fraction_of_safe_loans_that_are_exploratory


{(Finance.Initial_safe_assets-Government.initial_government_debt)*(1-initial_fraction_of_safe_loans_that_will_fail)*(1-Finance.initial_fraction_of_safe_loans_that_are_exploratory)

docs.title: Finance.Non_Energy_Bank_Investment
docs.text:
stock

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Finance.change_in_non_energy_bank_investment

FRIDA does NOT have a separate “energy bank investment” stock because energy capacity and energy system investments are handled in the Energy sector, not through bank balance sheets.

Bank lending and investment affects non-energy parts of the economy (firms, capital formation, production capacity) via the Non-Energy Bank Investment stock.

Initial value: 

Finance.initial_non_energy_bank_investment

docs.title: Finance.lending_standards
docs.text:
stock

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Finance.change_in_lending_standards

Lending standards is the actual stock variable representing how tight or loose bank credit conditions are at a given moment. This stock increases from change in lending standard, with an initial value of 1, so the model starts from a normalized baseline credit stance. This variable matters because it is the implemented constraint on lending behavior, not just a desired signal, and it feeds into the wider macro-financial system. When lending standards rise, banks become more restrictive, which reduces bank investment growth and can also increase firm failures through refinancing pressure. So this is one of the main transmission variables through which climate-driven financial stress spreads into investment, output, and employment.

Initial value: 

1

docs.title: Finance.Bank_Exploration_Orientation
docs.text:
stock

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Finance.increase_in_bank_exploration_orientation · Outflows: Finance.decline_in_bank_exploration_orientation

It represents how willing banks are to pursue high-risk, exploratory loans (e.g., very innovative, uncertain, or emerging technologies). It is a normalized dimensionless variable (0 to 1) where 0 → banks are fully conservative, avoid risky exploration. 1 → banks are aggressively exploring high-risk/high-return opportunities.

This orientation directly controls how much of total bank investment goes into exploratory (risky) loans.

Initial value: 

0.5

docs.title: Finance.increase_in_bank_exploration_orientation
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

Banks increase their risk appetite when the macroeconomic signals are strong specifically when investment growth > GDP growth.

Equation: 

Finance.effect_investment_and_GDP_difference_on_bank_exploration*(Finance.max_bank_exploration_orientation-Finance.Bank_Exploration_Orientation)/Finance.max_bank_exploration_orientation

docs.title: Finance.decline_in_bank_exploration_orientation
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

Banks pull back from exploratory activities when profits decline or when financial stress increases.

Equation: 

(Finance.effect_profit_decline_on_bank_exploration)*Finance.Bank_Exploration_Orientation

docs.text:

new investment that are not failing this year.

(1-failure rate) means a survived portions. The failure rate here is using the same variable as used in regular good investment flow because the question in this variant is not “are CB projects inherently better?” It is “what happens if the CB expands investment even when private finance would be cautious?”

Equation:

docs.title: Finance.indicated_lending_standards
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

Indicated lending standards is the level of lending standards banks would like to have, given current default conditions and prudential signals, before implementation delay is accounted for. It is included because FRIDA separates the desired bank stance from the actual implemented lending stance, which is good system-dynamics practice whenever institutions adjust gradually. This variable is shaped by the effects of risky-default conditions and safe-default conditions, so it synthesizes the bank sector’s desired prudential response before smoothing. That means it represents the immediate policy signal inside banks: how tight credit should be right now if they could adjust instantly.

Equation: 

(Finance.lending_standards*Finance.effect_of_safe_default_rate_on_lending_standards)+(Finance.max_change_in_lending_standards*Finance.effect_of_risky_default_rate_on_lending_standards)

docs.title: Finance.time_to_change_lending_standards
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.5 – 2

Equation: 

[1] = 1.14041208323877

docs.title: Finance.bank_investment_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run

It represents the actual bank investment growth. It accounts for (1) indicated bank investment growth due to long term solvency and reference bank investment growth, and (2) effect of lending standard on bank investment growth rate.

(1) with the indicated bank investment growth, we can know what is our baseline lending expansion trend, given solvency and long-run expectations.

(2) with the effect of lending standards, we can know given credit conditions, risk appetite, and short-term stress, whether we tighten or loosen lending beyond the structural signal.

FRIDA needs both layers because banks do not decide credit growth in one step.

Equation: 

Finance.indicated_bank_investment_growth_rate*Finance.effect_of_lending_standards_on_bank_investment_growth_rate

docs.title: Finance.perceived_defaults_of_risky_assets_for_interest_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the amount of risky-loan defaults that lenders think are “real credit risk,” after accounting for the fact that some losses are covered by insurance and some insurance payouts do not fully reassure markets.

This variable is the bank/market’s perceived net credit loss signal that is used specifically to adjust the risk premium (risky interest rate).

Equation: 

SMTH1(Finance.total_risky_asset_defaults-
(Private_Insurance.private_insurance_payouts + Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_insurance_payout + Government.government_insurance_payout)
*Finance.fraction_of_insurance_payouts_not_influencing_risk_premium, Finance.reporting_delay)

docs.title: Finance.reporting_delay
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.125 – 1.5

Equation: 

[1] = 0.475468387137114

docs.title: Finance.measured_default_rate_of_risky_assets_for_interest_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: Per Year · Dimensions: Run

This normalizes defaults: $10B defaults is small if assets are $1T but huge if assets are $50B Banks price rates, not volumes.

Equation: 

(Finance.perceived_defaults_of_risky_assets_for_interest_rate/Finance.risky_bank_assets)

docs.title: Finance.failure_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It is the final percentage of firms that fail (go bankrupt) in each period, based on climate damage, investment weakness, and baseline failures. It is a central variable because firm failures can: - destroy bank assets → reduce solvency - reduce GDP - reduce employment - amplify the climate–finance doom loop

Equation: 

MIN(1, MAX(Finance.failure_rate_floor,  Finance.indicated_failure_rate+(Finance.max_change_in_failure_rate*Finance.effect_of_lending_standards_on_failure_rate)))

docs.title: Finance.normal_failure_rate
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.01 – 0.08

It is a baseline rate at which firms fail in a healthy economy, without any climate-related damages.

Equation: 

[1] = 0.0366772752025615

docs.title: Finance.elasticity_of_desired_bank_growth_rate_to_changes_in_lending_standards
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0 – 10

Equation: 

[1] = 2.98312748185925

docs.title: Finance.indicated_bank_investment_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run

It represent the trend banks would follow if there were two tactical adjustment like lending standard.

"Given long-term investment reference (which also coming from long-term lending growth) AND the bank’s solvency condition (equity ratio gap), what growth rate should the bank use before considering short-term lending standards?"

Equation: 

Finance.reference_bank_investment_growth_rate*Finance.effect_of_equity_gap_on_indicated_bank_investment_growth_rate

docs.title: Finance.max_change_in_failure_rate
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.02 – 0.5

This is the maximum amount by which bank behavior is allowed to adjust the failure rate up or down. Banks can worsen firm failures by up to 7 percentage points. They cannot create catastrophic spikes beyond this limit This prevents numerical instability and unrealistic cascades

Equation: 

[1] = 0.0758533243476001

docs.title: Finance.indicated_risk_premium
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

It explains what the risk premium should be right now, given defaults. It combines baseline (initial risk premium), effect of current default conditions. This is a desired level, not what actually happens instantly.

Equation: 

MINSOFT(Finance.initial_risk_premium*Finance.effect_of_risky_defaults_rate_on_risk_premium,  Finance.max_indicated_risk_premium, Finance.tolerance_of_the_cap_on_indicated_risk_premium)

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_risky_defaults_rate_on_risk_premium
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

“How aggressively do lenders reprice risk when defaults deviate from normal?” Higher elasticity → more nervous markets.

Equation: 

(Finance.measured_default_rate_of_risky_assets_for_interest_rate/Finance.reference_default_rate)^Finance.elasticity_of_bankruptcy_rate_on_risk_premium

docs.title: Finance.elasticity_of_bankruptcy_rate_on_risk_premium
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 2.5 – 5

Equation: 

[1] = 2.96025826515849

docs.text:

docs.title: Finance.climate_adjusted_failure_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This is the current failure rate, given today’s climate conditions. “If loans were issued today, what would be the probability of failure under today’s climate stress?”

Equation: 

Finance.normal_failure_rate*Finance.effect_of_STA_on_failure_rate

docs.title: Finance.Investment_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

Adding CB investments as an additional investment stream. When CB investments happen, the model should “see” higher investment activity. This is treated as combined growth signal from bank investment and CB investment

Equation: 

TREND(Finance.Bank_Investment 
+ Government.government_run_public_investment
+ Circular_Flow.Central_Bank_public_investment, 1, Finance.initial_bank_investment_growth_rate)

docs.title: Finance.investment_to_GDP_growth_ratio
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

The economy becomes fragile when investment does not keep pace with GDP growth.

Equation: 

Finance.Investment_growth_rate//GDP.nominal_GDP_growth_rate

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_investment_to_gdp_ratio_on_failure_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This variable measures how much the firm failure rate increases when investment grows more slowly relative to GDP. If GDP is growing but investment is not keeping up, firms cannot replace depreciating capital, productivity growth slows, margins shrink, and more firms fail.

MAX 0 shift upward so the final effect never drops below 1. This means weak investment can increase failure rates, but strong investment does not decrease failure rates.

Equation: 

MAX(0, 1+Finance.sensitivity_of_failure_rate_to_investment_to_gdp_ratio*(Finance.investment_to_GDP_growth_ratio/INIT(Finance.investment_to_GDP_growth_ratio)-1))

docs.title: Finance.sensitivity_of_failure_rate_to_investment_to_gdp_ratio
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.1 – 0.5

This determines how strongly firm failures respond to investment shortfall. A higher sensitivity means even a small drop in investment/GDP ratio → big increase in failure rate

This 0.17 value means a 10% drop in investment relative to GDP → raises the failure rate by about 1.7%.

Equation: 

[1] = 0.170408256374284

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_STA_on_failure_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This variable captures how rising global temperature (STA — Surface Temperature Anomaly) increases the firm failure rate, gradually.

So: STA = 0 → effect = 1 (no additional failures) STA = 1°C → effect = 1 + sensitivity STA = 2°C → effect = 1 + 2sensitivity STA = 3°C → effect = 1 + 3sensitivity

Equation: 

IF (Climate.switch_finance_damage=0) AND TIME > Climate.climate_impact_stoptime THEN
PREVIOUS(SELF, 1)
ELSE
1+Finance.sensitivity_of_effect_of_sta_on_failure_rate*(Energy_Balance_Model.Surface_Temperature_Anomaly)

docs.title: Finance.sensitivity_of_effect_of_sta_on_failure_rate
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: 1/deg C · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.1 – 1.1

Equation: 

[1] = 0.566684064319974

docs.title: Finance.normal_investment
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

Normal investment is the total bank investment (energy and non energy investment) excluding Exploratory investment.

Equation: 

Finance.Bank_Investment-Finance.exploratory_bank_investment

docs.title: Finance.exploratory_premium
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 2 – 4

Equation: 

[1] = 2

docs.title: Finance.growth_rate_of_GDP_growth
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year^2 · Dimensions: Run

This takes the derivative (rate of change) of the above smoothed GDP growth trend. This tells the model not only how fast GDP is growing, but whether GDP growth itself is increasing or decreasing.

This is important because exploratory loans are very sensitive to economic slowdowns.

Equation: 

DERIVN(Finance.GDP_growth_rate_over_the_last_year, 1)

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_GDP_growth_on_exploratory_loan_failure
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This is important because exploratory loans are very sensitive to economic slowdowns.

If GDP growth derivative is negative (GDP growth slowing): e.g., DERIVN = –0.02 MIN(0, –0.02) = –0.02 –(–0.02) = +0.02

If GDP growth derivative is positive or zero: MIN(0, positive) = 0 Thus, the input to the effect is always positive when GDP is weakening, and zero when GDP is stable or improving.

It means, when GDP growth slows down, exploratory loans start to fail more often, and the rate of failure increases faster when GDP growth is falling sharply

Equation: 

(-MIN(0, Finance.normalized_growth_rate_of_GDP_growth))^Finance.sensitivity_of_exploratory_loan_failure_to_GDP_growth

docs.title: Finance.sensitivity_of_exploratory_loan_failure_to_GDP_growth
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.5 – 1.5

Equation: 

[1] = 1.04850929725399

docs.text:

When investment grows faster than GDP (investment > GDP real), banks interpret this as expansionary conditions and become more willing to explore.

docs.title: Finance.normal_exploration_rate
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.01 – 0.05

In a normal economy (with no stress and no excitement), banks typically allocate about 3.7% of their lending toward exploratory/high-risk loans.

Equation: 

0.0369117820963221

docs.title: Finance.converted_normal_exploration_fraction
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It is the corrected baseline fraction of lending that ensures that when the bank is in “normal exploration mode,” the true exploration share equals the intended normal exploration rate.

Equation: 

Finance.normal_exploration_rate/Finance.exploration_effect_midpoint

docs.title: Finance.exploration_effect_midpoint
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.8 – 1.6

In a normal economy (with no stress and no excitement), banks typically allocate about 3.7% of their lending toward exploratory/high-risk loans.

The midpoint (usually corresponding to orientation = 0.5) is not equal to 1. When orientation = 0.5 (normal attitude), the effect multiplier ≈ 1.2113, not 1. That means at normal risk-attitude, banks scale exploratory investment UP by a factor of 1.2113.

Equation: 

1.21134805959858

docs.title: Finance.GDP_growth_rate_over_the_last_year
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run

This is a smoothed version of last year’s GDP growth rate. GDP growth can be volatile month to month. Banks do not react to the “instant” GDP growth number. They respond to the recent trend, not the noise.

Equation: 

SMTH1(GDP.nominal_GDP_growth_rate, 1)

docs.title: Finance.initial_bank_investment_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0 – 0.2

Equation: 

[1] = 0.0836275699207085

docs.title: Finance.reference_bank_investment_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

A very long-run smoothed benchmark of how fast banks typically/ normaly increase investment over decades. It is the “trend” or “expected structural pace” of credit expansion.

With 80 years of bank reference formation time, banks' deep expectations about long-run lending growth shouldn’t change because of: 5-year cycles, temporary recessions, crises, climate shocks

Equation: 

SMTH1(Finance.bank_investment_growth_rate, Finance.bank_reference_formation_time, Finance.initial_bank_investment_growth_rate)

docs.title: Finance.bank_reference_formation_time
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: years · Dimensions: Run · Range: 20 – 100

docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 1 – 1.5

Average time it takes contracts to reset, rates to be repriced, refinancing to occur.

Equation: 

[1] = 1

docs.title: Finance.average_private_debt_safe_interest
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

It represents how much income the insurance reserve generates each year from being held in safe assets rather than sitting idle. This is important because it makes the insurance fund financially sustainable over time and links insurance capacity to macro-financial conditions, especially the safe interest environment.SMTH1( … , average_time_private_refinances_debt ) introduces a delay / smoothing, meaning private borrowers and lenders do not instantly adjust to policy rate changes.

Equation: 

SMTH1(Government.safe_interest, Finance.average_time_private_refinances_debt)

docs.title: Finance.average_time_private_refinances_debt
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: years · Dimensions: Run · Range: 2 – 4

Equation: 

[1] = 3

docs.title: Finance.default_rate_from_interest_rate_increase
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the additional loan default rate caused by rising interest rates. It

The equation embodies three realities: (1) Borrower stress begins only when interest rises above origination interest. (2) The stress grows nonlinearly (exponent). (3) Defaults from interest hikes cannot exceed a realistic limit (soft cap).

Equation: 

MINSOFT(((Finance.sensitivity_of_defaults_to_changes_in_risky_interest*
MAX(0,Finance.extra_risky_interest_above_interest_at_origination*Finance.time_to_measure_changes_in_risky_interest))^Finance.exponential_effect_of_interest_on_defaults)/Finance.time_to_measure_changes_in_risky_interest, Finance.maximum_default_rate_from_interest_rate_increases, Finance.tolerance_of_the_cap_on_default_rate_from_interest_rate_increases)

docs.title: Finance.extra_risky_interest_above_interest_at_origination
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

This measures how much today’s risky interest rate exceeds the interest rate that borrowers originally agreed to. If this is negative or zero, borrowers are not exposed to extra interest burden. If positive → borrowers face higher repayment burden, which raises defaults.

Equation: 

Finance.risky_interest-Finance.risky_interest_at_loan_origination_time

docs.title: Finance.sensitivity_of_defaults_to_changes_in_risky_interest
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 10 – 13.3

This parameter determines how strongly borrowers respond when interest increases. High sensitivity → even small interest increases create many defaults Low sensitivity → borrowers are more resilient It is a scaling factor before the exponential effect

Equation: 

[1] = 11.89928999

docs.title: Finance.exponential_effect_of_interest_on_defaults
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 8 – 12

This exponent controls nonlinearity. Why exponent? Because empirical credit data shows: A small interest increase → small default increase A big interest increase → disproportionately large default increase. So this exponent captures this non-linear jump in risk.

Equation: 

9.049245183

docs.title: Finance.time_to_measure_changes_in_risky_interest
docs.text:
aux Unit helper

Units: year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.8 – 1.2

This is to keep the units consistent with the exponential form of the equation

Equation: 

[1] = 1

docs.title: Finance.conversion_rate_from_interest_rate_decrease
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

If extra risky interest above interest at origination is positive, interest rates have increased since the loan was issued. If this is negative, interest rates have decreased since origination. In this variable, we focus on the negative case.

If interest decreased, then: extra_risky_interest_above_interest_at_origination < 0 multiply by –1 → positive MAX(0, positive) → positive So this logic captures the magnitude of interest rate decreases.

Equation: 

MINSOFT(((Finance.sensitivity_of_defaults_to_changes_in_risky_interest*
MAX(0,-1*Finance.extra_risky_interest_above_interest_at_origination*Finance.time_to_measure_changes_in_risky_interest))^Finance.exponential_effect_of_interest_on_defaults)
/Finance.time_to_measure_changes_in_risky_interest, Finance.maximum_default_rate_from_interest_rate_increases, Finance.tolerance_of_the_cap_on_default_rate_from_interest_rate_increases)

docs.title: Finance.max_indicated_risk_premium
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

Upper bound on how high spreads can go. Markets panic, but not infinitely.

Equation: 

Finance.max_risk_premium_increase_rate+Finance.Risk_Premium

docs.title: Finance.max_risk_premium_increase_rate
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.01 – 0.15

Limits how fast spreads can rise. Prevents instantaneous spikes.

Equation: 

0.015

docs.title: Finance.maximum_default_rate_from_interest_rate_increases
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.1 – 0.5

maximum value to prevent unrealistic spikes where all loans default immediately.

In real financial systems not all loans default instantly, even when interest spikes. Some have fixed rates. Some refinance.

Equation: 

.15

docs.title: Finance.tolerance_of_the_cap_on_default_rate_from_interest_rate_increases
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.01 – 0.11

tolerance_of_the_cap lets the cap be smoothly applied rather than abruptly.

Equation: 

0.06

docs.title: Finance.tolerance_of_the_cap_on_indicated_risk_premium
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.005 – 0.015

Softens the cap (smooth clipping instead of hard cutoff). This avoids numerical and behavioral discontinuities.

Equation: 

0.01

docs.title: Finance.Bank_Investment
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

This is a backward-looking historical measure of how the stock of investment itself has been growing over time. It looks at how Bank Investment has changed in the past.

NOTE: The different with bank investment growth rate is that bank investment growth rate is a forward-looking behavioral decision made by banks based on: solvency (equity gap → indicated rate) and short-term lending standards

Equation: 

Finance.Nominal_Gross_Energy_Investment+Finance.Non_Energy_Bank_Investment

docs.title: Finance.private_investment
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

In FRIDA, private investment is driven by bank investment, because firms borrow from banks, banks decide how much to invest/loan. This is consistent with stock-flow consistent macro models and Schumpeterian finance-driven growth.

Private investment does not jump immediately when bank investment changes. It changes smoothly over 1 year.

Private investment feeds into circular flow firm as an inflow. It is the investment flow that the economy sees. Therefore, adding CB investment in this variable aims to allow other part of economy 'sees' this impact of investment (e.g. employment).

Equation: 

SMTH1(Finance.Bank_Investment, 1, Finance.initial_non_energy_bank_investment)

docs.title: Finance.stranded_energy_capital
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the value of fossil-energy-related capital that has lost economic value due to climate policy or energy transition.

Examples of stranded capital: - Coal plants shut down early - Oil/gas reserves that cannot be extracted - Fossil infrastructure that becomes uneconomic

Equation: 

fossil_energy.stranded_capital*Inflation."inflation_adjustment_b$_per_bc$"/Finance."UNIT_c$_per_Bc$"

docs.title: Finance.stranded_energy_related_capital
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

This captures second-order losses where stranded fossil assets don’t just lose value themselves. They also pull down the value of related assets, such as: - pipelines - fossil transport fleets - fossil-dependent industrial supply chains

This variable multiplies the stranded capital by a factor (effect_of_stranded_energy_capital_on_other_assets), which represents: “For every $1 of fossil stranded capital, how much additional asset value is lost elsewhere in the system?”

For example, if the factor = 0.5: $100B stranded fossil assets create $50B additional losses in related sectors. This is realistic: entire value chains collapse when fossil infrastructure is stranded.

Equation: 

Finance.stranded_energy_capital*Finance.effect_of_stranded_energy_capital_on_other_assets

docs.title: Finance.stranded_energy_capital_reference_formation_time
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: years · Dimensions: Run · Range: 10 – 110

Equation: 

50

docs.title: Finance.sensitivity_of_effect_of_stranded_energy_capital_on_other_assets
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 1 – 5

Equation: 

2.15638540234467

docs.title: Finance.minimum_reference_stranded_energy_capital
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.5 – 2.5

Equation: 

1.5

docs.title: Finance.stranded_capital_related_defaults
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

The number of safe loans that default because of stranded assets is the minimum of: (a) The maximum possible defaults banks can suffer: A fraction of safe loans is exposed to the fossil sector, maximized by safe loans default caused by stranded capital. (b) The actual economic loss due to stranded assets.

MIN function because banks cannot lose more safe loans than are exposed to fossil risks AND banks cannot lose more than the real stranded asset losses.

Equation: 

MIN(Finance.Safe_Loans*Finance.max_safe_loan_defaults_caused_by_stranded_capital, Finance.stranded_energy_capital + Finance.stranded_energy_related_capital)

docs.title: Finance.equity_ratio_gap
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

Signals whether banks feel safe or unsafe, and therefore whether they expand or contract lending.

If desired > actual A/L ratio → gap is positive → bank is under-solvent relative to target → bank wants to become safer → reduces lending

If desired < actual A/L ratio → gap is negative → bank is more than safe relative to target → bank can expand lending

Equation: 

Finance.bank_desired_asset_to_liability_ratio-Finance.assets_to_liability_ratio

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_equity_ratio_gap_on_investment_growth_when_above_target
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This node represents lending expansion effect If banks are overcapitalized → investment growth is stimulated.

Equation: 

EXPBOUND(Finance.equity_gap_above_min_effect, Finance.equity_gap_zero_effect_y, Finance.equity_ratio_gap, Finance.equity_ratio_gap_effect_above_exponent, -Finance.equity_gap_effect_max_x_magnitude,  Finance.equity_gap_effect_zero_effect_x)

docs.title: Finance.equity_gap_above_min_effect
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: -50 – 0.5

Equation: 

0.433728485607813

docs.title: Finance.equity_ratio_gap_effect_above_exponent
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: -10 – -1

Equation: 

-1.13226853323907

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_equity_ratio_gap_on_investment_growth_when_below_target
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This node represents lending contraction effect. If banks are undercapitalized → investment growth is restricted. Purpose: This is how solvency problems propagate into the real economy.

Equation: 

EXPBOUND(Finance.equity_gap_zero_effect_y, Finance.equity_gap_below_max_effect, Finance.equity_ratio_gap, Finance.equity_ratio_gap_effect_below_exponent, Finance.equity_gap_effect_zero_effect_x, Finance.equity_gap_effect_max_x_magnitude)

docs.title: Finance.equity_gap_below_max_effect
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 1.2 – 10

Equation: 

3.10546015389887

docs.title: Finance.equity_ratio_gap_effect_below_exponent
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 1 – 10

Equation: 

1.07079537366798

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_equity_gap_on_indicated_bank_investment_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This is the behavioral driver that then feeds into the indicated bank investment growth. It tells us “How much should banks increase or decrease their investment growth because of their solvency (equity ratio) situation?”

  • 1 when banks feel perfectly solvent (no pressure to adjust investment or lending)
  • less than 1 when banks feel unsafe (need to contract investment or lending)

Equation: 

Finance.effect_of_equity_ratio_gap_on_investment_growth_when_above_target*Finance.effect_of_equity_ratio_gap_on_investment_growth_when_below_target

docs.title: Finance.equity_gap_effect_zero_effect_x
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl

0 is no effect normalization.

Defines the value of equity ratio gap where the effect on investment growth is zero (i.e., neutral position). When banks are exactly at target solvency → no pressure to shrink or expand lending.

Equation: 

0

docs.title: Finance.equity_gap_effect_max_x_magnitude
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.05 – 0.2

Defines the maximum deviation of the effect function. This restricts how strong banks can react when gap is extreme (e.g., cannot cut lending infinitely).

Equation: 

0.169777247127836

docs.title: Finance.equity_gap_zero_effect_y
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl

1 to 1 normalization

Defines the value of equity ratio gap where the effect on investment growth is zero (i.e., neutral position). When banks are exactly at target solvency → no pressure to shrink or expand lending.

Equation: 

1

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_lending_standards_on_failure_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This is a behavioral multiplier based on short-term credit conditions.

If banks tighten lending standards, this multiplier increases → more firms fail If banks loosen lending, this multiplier decreases → fewer failures.

Banks tightening lending increases defaults because firms cannot refinance working capital, repay debts, or roll over liabilities.

Equation: 

LOGISTICBOUND(Finance.effect_of_lending_standards_on_failure_rate_range/2, -Finance.effect_of_lending_standards_on_failure_rate_range/2, Finance.lending_standards, 1,  Finance.effect_of_lending_standards_on_failure_rate_curvature)

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_lending_standards_on_failure_rate_range
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.5 – 10

Equation: 

2.95036620976155

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_lending_standards_on_failure_rate_curvature
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 1 – 10

Equation: 

5.91542891023683

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_risky_default_rate_on_lending_standards
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It tells given how risky-loan defaults are behaving relative to their normal level, how much should banks tighten (or loosen) lending standards. It is designed to be centered around 0 change at baseline, and capped, so banks cannot tighten/loosen infinitely.

Equation: 

LOGISTICBOUND(-Finance.effect_of_default_rate_on_lending_standards_range/2, Finance.effect_of_default_rate_on_lending_standards_range/2, Finance.measured_default_rate_of_risky_assets_for_lending_standards/INIT(Finance.measured_default_rate_of_risky_assets_for_lending_standards), 1,  Finance.effect_of_default_rate_on_lending_standards_curvature)

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_default_rate_on_lending_standards_range
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.5 – 5

Equation: 

1.94742064616041

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_default_rate_on_lending_standards_curvature
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 1 – 10

Equation: 

4.00407024369583

docs.title: Finance.indicated_failure_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This is the baseline failure rate BEFORE banks tighten lending. It is the climate-adjusted or economic-conditions-adjusted default rate.

It already includes: - normal failure rate (economic turnover) - effect of STA (temperature-induced failures) - effect of investment-to-GDP ratio (weak investment → more failures)

Equation: 

Finance.climate_adjusted_failure_rate*Finance.effect_of_investment_to_gdp_ratio_on_failure_rate

docs.title: Finance.reference_default_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run

“What default rate is considered normal?” This anchors pricing behavior.

Equation: 

SMTH1(Finance.measured_default_rate_of_risky_assets_for_interest_rate, Finance.bank_reference_formation_time)

docs.title: Finance.perceived_defaults_of_safe_assets_not_caused_by_energy_transition
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

What banks believe are “normal” credit losses on safe assets, after filtering out losses that are clearly attributable to the energy transition (stranded assets).

It is perceived, not actual → it reflects reported, interpreted losses.

Equation: 

SMTH1(Finance.safe_loan_defaults+Finance.actual_safe_investment_failure-Finance.stranded_capital_related_defaults, Finance.reporting_delay)

docs.title: Finance.measured_default_rate_of_safe_assets
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the fraction of the “safe” loan portfolio that banks perceive as defaulting.

Equation: 

Finance.perceived_defaults_of_safe_assets_not_caused_by_energy_transition/Finance.private_safe_bank_assets

docs.title: Finance.effect_of_safe_default_rate_on_lending_standards
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It represents how much banks tighten or loosen lending standards in response to defaults on assets they believe are “safe.

It is a multiplier applied to lending standards. Value = 1 → no change in lending standards Value > 1 → banks tighten lending Value < 1 → banks loosen lending

Safe-loan defaults are treated as a clear prudential trigger. FRIDA uses a straightforward proportional rule rather than a bounded logistic response, effectively saying if even safe assets are defaulting more than normal, banks adjust standards directly.

Equation: 

1+ Finance.sensitivity_of_effect_of_safe_default_rate_on_lending_standards*(Finance.measured_default_rate_of_safe_assets/Finance.safe_asset_default_rate_threshold-1)

docs.title: Finance.sensitivity_of_effect_of_safe_default_rate_on_lending_standards
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.01 – 0.2

Equation: 

0.150971040563369

docs.title: Finance.safe_asset_default_rate_threshold
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0 – 0.05

This is the reference level of default that banks consider “normal” for safe assets.

Equation: 

0.015

docs.title: Finance.normal_rate_of_converting_safe_loans_to_safe_loans_for_financing_innovation
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.02 – 0.04

This is a baseline conversion speed. It answers: “If firms were fully innovation-oriented, how fast could banks normally shift safe lending toward innovation?”

It reflects: - institutional capacity, - regulatory acceptance, - banking routines, - market familiarity with innovation finance.

Equation: 

0.03

docs.title: Finance.rate_of_converting_safe_loans_to_safe_loans_for_financing_innovation
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run

It represents the rate at which banks reallocate existing safe loans toward financing innovation, scaled by firms’ innovation orientation, ensuring that innovation finance expands only when both banking capacity and firm-level innovation demand are present.

MAX(0, …) ensures the rate is never negative. Banks cannot “un-convert” loans via this channel. The flow only exists when innovation demand exists. If innovation orientation is zero or negative → conversion rate = 0.

Equation: 

MAX(0, Finance.normal_rate_of_converting_safe_loans_to_safe_loans_for_financing_innovation*Innovation.Firms_Innovation_Orientation)

docs.title: Finance.total_climate_caused_risky_defaults
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It represents the total annual volume of risky-loan defaults in the economy that are specifically attributable to climate damage, and it is the loss base from which policy interventions are calculated.

Equation: 

Finance.bad_loans_defaults*Finance.fraction_of_defaults_that_are_caused_by_climate

docs.title: Finance.fraction_of_defaults_that_are_caused_by_climate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It addresses if the risk embedded in these loans when they were issued, what share was already climate-related.

This avoids falsely attributing normal business failures, macroeconomic failures to climate change.

Equation: 

(Finance.climate_adjusted_failure_rate_when_default_originated-Finance.normal_failure_rate)/Finance.climate_adjusted_failure_rate_when_default_originated

docs.title: Finance.climate_adjusted_failure_rate_when_default_originated
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It represents the historical failure environment embedded in the current loan portfolio. "What failure rate was in effect when the loans that are defaulting today were originally issued?"

Loans that default today were not created today because they were created many years ago, under different climate and economic conditions. It allows default dynamics to depend on the worsening of conditions relative to origination rather than instantaneous climate risk. Because loans last many years, today’s defaults come from loans issued under past climate conditions.

Equation: 

SMTH1(Finance.climate_adjusted_failure_rate, Finance.loan_maturation_time)

docs.title: Finance.perceived_defaults_of_risky_assets_for_lending_standards
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

This variable is the amount of risky-asset defaults that banks perceive when deciding whether to tighten or loosen lending standards. Its equation is SMTH1(total risky asset defaults - (private insurance payouts + Central Bank insurance payout +government insurance payout), reporting delay), so it takes actual risky defaults, subtracts insurance coverage, and then smooths the result through a reporting delay. This matters because banks do not react to raw defaults instantly or perfectly; they react to a delayed and partially insured loss signal. The variable is therefore the behavioral credit-stress input used for prudential tightening, rather than a direct accounting measure of defaults.

Equation: 

SMTH1(Finance.total_risky_asset_defaults -
(Private_Insurance.private_insurance_payouts +Central_Bank_Policies.Central_Bank_insurance_payout + Government.government_insurance_payout),
 Finance.reporting_delay)

docs.title: Finance.measured_default_rate_of_risky_assets_for_lending_standards
docs.text:
aux

Units: Per Year · Dimensions: Run

From the bank’s point of view, what fraction of our risky assets appears to be defaulting, after accounting for insurance and with reporting delays. So this is a rate (share), not a volume. Banks use perceived default rate to decide whether to tighten or loosen lending standards.This variable converts the perceived volume of risky defaults into a default rate that can be used in the lending-standards rule. In other words, instead of reacting to defaults in absolute b$/year, banks react to a normalized measure of how severe risky defaults are relative to the relevant risky-asset base. I

Equation: 

(Finance.perceived_defaults_of_risky_assets_for_lending_standards/Finance.risky_bank_assets)

docs.title: Finance.fraction_of_insurance_payouts_not_influencing_risk_premium
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.25 – 0.75

Only 50% of insurance payouts reduce the risk-premium-relevant perception of defaults. This is a behavioral realism assumption: insurance dampens stress, but does not eliminate perceived risk.

Equation: 

Government_Regulations.fraction_of_loans_insured

docs.title: Finance.initial_CB_good_loans
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: b$ · Dimensions: Run · Range: 7500 – 8500

Equation: 

0

docs.title: Finance.initial_CB_bad_loans
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: b$ · Dimensions: Run · Range: 7500 – 8500

Equation: 

0

docs.title: Finance.rate_of_converting_CB_safe_loans_to_CB_safe_loans_for_financing_innovation
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run

It represents the rate at which banks reallocate existing safe loans toward financing innovation, scaled by firms’ innovation orientation, ensuring that innovation finance expands only when both banking capacity and firm-level innovation demand are present.

MAX(0, …) ensures the rate is never negative. Banks cannot “un-convert” loans via this channel. The flow only exists when innovation demand exists. If innovation orientation is zero or negative → conversion rate = 0.

Equation: 

MAX(0, Finance.normal_rate_of_converting_safe_loans_to_safe_loans_for_financing_innovation*Innovation.Firms_Innovation_Orientation)

docs.title: GDP.Average_GDP
docs.text:
stock

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: GDP.change_in_average_GDP

Average GDP is a smoothed stock, a running average of GDP over time.

Many behaviors depend on trend GDP, not instantaneous GDP Smoothing removes noise from year-to-year shocks.

Nominal GDP = “What is happening this year?” Average GDP = “What is happening on average over the past few years?”

Initial value: 

GDP.initial_average_GDP

docs.title: GDP.Cumulative_Discount_Exponent
docs.text:
stock

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: GDP.discount_rate_accumulation

The stock is just a memory of how much discounting has accumulated so far. Because safe interest rate is dynamic, it changes over time in the model. This stock answers: “How much total discounting has built up since 2030/ NPV start year?” The initial value is 0 because before policy start year/ 2030, we do not want any accumulated discounting yet.

Initial value: 

0

docs.title: GDP.NPV_of_Real_GDP
docs.text:
stock

Units: bc$ · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: GDP.discounted_real_GDP

Cumulative net present value of real GDP from the NPV evaluation start year to the end of the simulation.

Initial value: 

0

docs.title: GDP.Cumulative_Welfare_Discount_Exponent
docs.text:
stock

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: GDP.welfare_discount_rate_accumulation

This stock keeps track of how much discounting has built up since 2030. Initial value is 0 because there is no discounted accumulated yet. Because safe interest rate is dynamic, it changes over time in the model. This stock answers: “How much total discounting has built up since 2030/ welfare start year?”

Initial value: 

0

docs.title: GDP.Discounted_Welfare_of_Real_GDP
docs.text:
stock

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: GDP.discounted_welfare

Cumulative welfare from the objective evaluation start year to the end of the simulation.

Initial value: 

0

docs.title: GDP.accumulating_real_gdp_growth_rate_deviation_from_average_in_calibration_period
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

Equation: 

IF TIME >= GDP.real_gdp_calibration_data_start_year THEN
ABS(GDP.average_real_gdp_growth_rate_in_calibration_period-GDP.real_GDP_growth_rate)
ELSE
0

docs.title: GDP.discount_rate_accumulation
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

Instantaneous discount rate applied from the NPV evaluation start year onward.

Equation: 

IF TIME >= GDP.objective_evaluation_start_year
THEN GDP.ramp_discount_rate
ELSE 0

docs.title: GDP.discounted_real_GDP
docs.text:
flow

Units: bc$/year · Dimensions: Run

Real GDP in 2021 prices, discounted back to the NPV base (effectively from NPV evaluation start year in this setup).

Equation: 

IF TIME >= GDP.objective_evaluation_start_year
THEN GDP.NPV_discount_factor * GDP."Real_GDP_in_2021c$"
ELSE 0

docs.title: GDP.welfare_discount_rate_accumulation
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

Instantaneous discount rate applied from the welfare evaluation start year onward. Before 2030, no welfare discounting and from 2030 onward, discount using the safe interest rate.

Equation: 

IF TIME >= GDP.objective_evaluation_start_year
THEN GDP.ramp_discount_rate
ELSE 0

docs.title: GDP.discounted_welfare
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the welfare contribution in each year after applying the concave transformation and applying the discount factor.

Equation: 

IF TIME >= GDP.objective_evaluation_start_year
THEN (GDP.instantaneous_welfare_from_real_GDP*GDP.welfare_discount_factor) / GDP.time_unit_correction
ELSE 0

docs.title: GDP.initial_nominal_GDP_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0 – 0.1

Equation: 

0.0414868952469566

docs.title: GDP.Nominal_GDP
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

Nominal GDP is the value of all final goods and services produced, measured in current dollars (the dollar value of that year).

This reflects current-year prices, showing the total economic activity in money terms (private consumption, total investment, and government consumption)

Equation: 

Circular_Flow.private_consumption + Finance.total_investment + Government.government_consumption

docs.title: GDP."Real_GDP_in_2021c$"
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: bc$/year · Dimensions: Run

Real GDP remove inflation, here uses 2021 dollars as the reference (base year), allowing us to compare actual economic output over time, independent of inflation.

It reflects how much the economy actually produces, measured in the purchasing power of 2021.

Equation: 

GDP.Nominal_GDP/Inflation."inflation_adjustment_b$_per_bc$"

docs.title: GDP.initial_real_GDP_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0 – 0.05

Equation: 

[1] = 0.0236863127624338

docs.title: GDP.current_recession_time_counter
docs.text:
aux

Units: Year · Dimensions: Run

Equation: 

IF TIME > Government_Regulations.start_year AND GDP.real_GDP_growth_rate < GDP.negative_growth_threshold THEN
PREVIOUS(SELF, 0) + DT
ELSE 0

docs.title: GDP.future_time_in_recession
docs.text:
aux

Units: Year · Dimensions: Run

Equation: 

IF TIME > Government_Regulations.start_year THEN 
PREVIOUS(SELF, 0) + (GDP.real_GDP_growth_rate < 0) * DT
ELSE 
0

docs.title: GDP.historical_nominal_GDP_growth_rate_explosion_stopper
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

Equation: 

IF TIME <= Government_Regulations.start_year AND
(GDP.nominal_GDP_growth_rate > .3 OR GDP.nominal_GDP_growth_rate < -0.3) 
THEN
5/0
ELSE
0

docs.title: GDP.objective_evaluation_start_year
docs.text:
aux

Units: Year

It is to define the year when the composite policy objective begins to be evaluated.

Equation: 

2030

docs.title: GDP.NPV_discount_factor
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This is the continuous-time discount factor based on the accumulated safe interest rate.

Equation: 

EXP(-GDP.Cumulative_Discount_Exponent)

docs.title: GDP.instantaneous_welfare_from_real_GDP
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This variable converts GDP into a welfare score. Each year’s welfare is the log of real GDP in 2021 prices (diminishing marginal welfare of extra GDP). Higher GDP still means higher welfare, but extra GDP has smaller additional value.

Equation: 

LN(MAX(GDP."Real_GDP_in_2021c$", 1e-06))

docs.title: GDP.welfare_discount_factor
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This tells the model how much to discount future welfare each year, using the safe interest rate from the Government sector. This gives the shrinking weight over time: at 2030, factor = 1. Later, factor gets smaller so later welfare counts less.

Equation: 

EXP(-GDP.Cumulative_Welfare_Discount_Exponent)

docs.title: Government.Government_Debt
docs.text:
stock

Units: B$ · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Government.government_taking_up_debt, Government.government_insurance_payout, Government.bailouts · Outflows: Government.insurance_tax_redemption

It is the stock of accumulated public debt.

Initial value: 

Government.initial_government_debt

docs.title: Government.Diagnostic_Government_Insurance_Reserve
docs.text:
stock

Units: b$ · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Government.revenue_from_tax_redemption · Outflows: Government.payout

It is a diagnostic stock that tracks the cumulative balance of the insurance tax and insurance payout system. This stock is perceived as a fund balance signal. This stock rises when taxes exceed payouts and falls when payouts exceed taxes. This is useful because it gives the model a reserve-gap indicator for tax adjustment. It is called "diagnostic" because it does not constrain government spending and it is not the real reserve. It only records what the balance would be if the insurance tax were a dedicated funding stream.

Initial value: 

0

docs.title: Government.government_insurance_taxation_rate
docs.text:
stock

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Government.change_in_government_insurance_taxation_rate

This stock is the actual implemented insurance tax rate applied to owner savings. It is a stock, not an instant converter, because the model assumes tax policy adjusts gradually rather than jumping immediately to the desired level. This variable represents the real policy rate in force at any given time, after administrative and political adjustment delays.

Initial value: 

0

docs.title: Government.government_insurance_payout
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the flow that represents how much climate-caused risky defaults that the government has to cover every year once the policy active. It serves two key purposes in the wider model: (1) Fiscal or accounting: it enters the government budget and debt accumulation and (2) Financial stability: it reduces perceived risky defaults and lending standards

Equation: 

Finance.total_climate_caused_risky_defaults
* Government_Regulations.fraction_of_default_covered_by_government_insurance

docs.title: Government.insurance_tax_redemption
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is included because if the government is insuring climate-related defaults, there should also be a funding stream that partially finances that support. This variable reduces Government Debt in the debt equation and also enters the diagnostic stock as revenue. Conceptually, it is the fiscal counterpart to government insurance payouts.

Equation: 

Circular_Flow.insurance_taxation

docs.title: Government.revenue_from_tax_redemption
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is simply the inflow from insurance tax redemption into the diagnostic reserve stock. Because it is equal to insurance-tax receipts, it represents how much funding the policy collects each year from the dedicated insurance tax.

Equation: 

Government.insurance_tax_redemption

docs.title: Government.payout
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the outflow from the diagnostic reserve stock, and it is defined as government insurance payout. If payout is larger than revenue for a long time, the diagnostic reserve will trend downward, indicating under-taxation relative to insurance burden. So this variable is the expenditure side of the policy-funding feedback

Equation: 

Government.government_insurance_payout

docs.title: Government.change_in_government_insurance_taxation_rate
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year^2 · Dimensions: Run

It s the rate at which the actual insurance tax moves toward the bounded indicated tax rate. This is included to capture implementation delay, governments do not instantly change tax policy to the new desired level. From the equation, a larger gap between desired and actual tax rates produces faster adjustment, while a longer taxation rate adjustment time slows the response.

Equation: 

IF Government_Regulations.fraction_of_default_covered_by_government_insurance > 0
THEN ((Government.bounded_indicated_government_insurance_taxation_rate - Government.government_insurance_taxation_rate) / Government.taxation_rate_adjustment_time)
ELSE ((0 - Government.government_insurance_taxation_rate) / Government.taxation_rate_adjustment_time)

docs.title: Government.change_in_government_public_investment_policy
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year/Year · Dimensions: Run

Purpose: the implemented additional public investment flow (ramps in/out smoothly)

Equation: 

(Government.public_investments_policy_target - Government.Government_Run_Public_Investment_Policy) / Government.time_to_implement_public_investment_policy

docs.title: Government.government_consumption
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

government consumption as the sum of three separate components, each representing a different type of spending. (1) STA driven government consumption, consumption that increases because of climate damages, specifically those driven by Surface Temperature Anomaly (STA), e.g., emergency response, reconstruction, adaptation, public services, etc. (2) non climate driven government consumption, normal government consumption that would exist even without climate change, including education, heatlhcare, administration, defense, etc. (3) Paid total nominal SLR protection cost, these are the nominal (not inflation-adjusted) costs the government must pay for Sea-Level-Rise (SLR) protection, e.g., sea walls, coastal defense infrastrucutre, land elevation, etc.

Equation: 

Government.STA_driven_government_consumption+Government.non_climate_driven_government_consumption+Government.Paid_Total_Nominal_SLR_protection_cost

docs.title: Government.government_transfers
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

The government cannot spend unlimited money on transfers. Transfers = the smaller of what government can afford and what society needs.

This means, if needs exceed the budget, transfers are rationed. If budget exceeds needs, only actual needs are paid.

Equation: 

MIN(Government.public_expenditure_before_subsidies_and_interest_payments*Government.maximum_percentage_of_government_budget_to_transfer_payments, (Government.transfer_payments_for_children+Government.transfer_payments_for_retirees+Government.transfer_payments_for_unemployed)/Government."UNIT_$_per_B$")
