docs.title: Government.MAX_insurance_taxation_rate
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.01 – 0.05

It is the upper bound on the government insurance tax rate. It is included because even if the reserve gap becomes very large, the government cannot realistically impose an unlimited tax increase. This cap keeps the policy controller within a plausible range and avoids explosive or politically impossible tax settings. Note: Wealth tax is 5% and we are using half of it.

Equation: 

0.02

docs.title: Government.MIN_insurance_taxation_rate
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.001 – 0.005

It is the lower bound on the government insurance tax rate. It is included so that the policy controller cannot push the tax below a floor that would be unrealistic or inconsistent with the intended policy design. This floor also helps avoid unstable behavior in which the rate collapses to near zero even though the insurance system may still need a funding base. So it acts as a stabilizing lower boundary for the tax instrument.

Equation: 

0.001

docs.title: Government.taxation_rate_adjustment_time
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: years · Dimensions: Run · Range: 1 – 5

It is the time constant governing how quickly the government can implement changes in the insurance tax rate. A short time value means the government can adjust the tax relatively quickly, while a long value means the actual tax rate lags behind the desired bounded rate for longer. In this model, we use 2 years as the value.

Equation: 

2

docs.title: Government.indicated_government_insurance_taxation_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the raw tax rate suggested by the reserve-gap feedback before any bounds are applied. It exists because the model first computes what tax rate would be desirable given the current reserve imbalance, and only afterward constrains that value to feasible limits. If the reserve gap is positive, the indicated rate rises; if the reserve position is stronger than needed, the indicated rate falls.

Equation: 

(Government.government_insurance_payout+Government.reserve_correction_flow)/
MAX(Circular_Flow.owner_savings, 0.000001)

docs.title: Government.government_insurance_reserve_gap
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$ · Dimensions: Run

It measures the difference between the desired reserve position and the current diagnostic reserve position. It exists because the model uses a feedback controller: A positive gap means the system is underfunded relative to its target and should push the tax rate upward, while a negative gap means the system is over-funded and can ease the tax rate.

Equation: 

Government.target_reserve_gap-Government.Diagnostic_Government_Insurance_Reserve

docs.title: Government.target_reserve_gap
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$

It is the desired benchmark for the government insurance reserve gap. It is included because the model needs a reference level against which the actual reserve balance can be judged. In control-theoretic terms, this is the target condition the tax policy is trying to maintain. The value is zero meaning that there is no gap desired.

Equation: 

0

docs.title: Government.reserve_correction_flow
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the correction setting used when reserve gap are larger or when a quicker response is needed. Compared with reserve correction time, this parameter makes the indicated tax rate more responsive to reserve shortfalls. So it helps the controller react more aggressively when the diagnostic reserve signal suggests the policy is materially off target

Equation: 

Government.government_insurance_reserve_gap/Government.reserve_correction_time

docs.title: Government.reserve_correction_time
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: Year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 2 – 10

It is a parameter governing how strongly the tax system responds when reserve imbalances are modest or when adjustment is intended to be gradual. It is included because governments generally do not jump tax rates sharply in response to every short-run reserve fluctuation. A slower correction parameter produces a more cautious response, reducing volatility in the policy rate. In effect, it shapes the smoothness and political realism of the insurance tax adjustment process.

Equation: 

5

docs.title: Government.bounded_indicated_government_insurance_taxation_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the policy-recommended tax rate after applying lower and upper limits. This is included to prevent unrealistic tax outcomes, such as negative tax rates or implausibly high emergency taxation.

Equation: 

MIN(MAX(Government.indicated_government_insurance_taxation_rate, Government.MIN_insurance_taxation_rate),  Government.MAX_insurance_taxation_rate)

docs.title: Inflation.Inflation_Index
docs.text:
stock

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Inflation.change_in_inflation_index

Inflation index (often called a price index or CPI index) is a cumulative measure of how much price have changed over time. E.g., if the inflation index = 1.2, the price today is 20% higher than in the base year.

“What is the overall price level today compared to the base year?”

it is used to compute the real GDP

Initial value: 

Inflation.initial_inflation_index

docs.title: Inflation.Inflation_Rate
docs.text:
stock

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Inflation.inflationary_pressure · Outflows: Inflation.deflationary_pressure

Inflation rate is the percentage change in prices per year. If it is > 0, then the prices are rising. If it is < 0, the prices are falling (deflation).

“How fast are prices increasing right now?”

Initial value: 

Inflation.initial_inflation_rate

docs.title: Inflation.change_in_inflation_index
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year · Dimensions: Run

The inflation index increases at a rate equal to the inflation rate times the current index.

Inflation changes the current price level, not the base year price level. Inflation is exponential (compounding), not linear.

Equation: 

Inflation.Inflation_Rate*Inflation.Inflation_Index

{IF TIME > 2020 THEN inflation_percentage ELSE 0,1-RAMP(,002; STARTTIME)

docs.title: Inflation.inflationary_pressure
docs.text:
flow

Units: 1/year/Year · Dimensions: Run

If the inflation gap is positive, inflation must rise. The rise is gradual, over the inflation_adjustment_time. Only positive gaps create inflationary pressure.

The MAX(0, …) ensures equation does not treat a negative gap as “negative pressure”; that is handled separately.

Equation: 

MAX(0, Inflation.inflation_gap/Inflation.inflation_adjustment_time)

docs.title: Inflation.deflationary_pressure
docs.text:
flow

Units: dmnl/year^2 · Dimensions: Run

If the inflation gap is negative, inflation should fall. This negative adjustment happens gradually. Only negative gaps trigger deflation.

MIN(0, …) isolates the negative part. The negative sign makes the outflow positive.

Equation: 

-MIN(0, Inflation.inflation_gap/Inflation.deflation_adjustment_time)

docs.title: Inflation.aggregate_income
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the total income available to the whole economy (all sectors combined). It includes income generated and paid by the private sector AND net injections or withdrawals from the government sector

Equation: 

Inflation.private_income+Inflation.net_government_expenses

docs.title: Inflation.net_government_expenses
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

Net government expenses represent the government’s net injection or withdrawal of money into the private economy. When it spends → it injects income. When it taxes → it withdraws income When it provides bailouts → it injects additional income

If government_spending > tax_income → government is injecting money → aggregate income rises.

Equation: 

Government.public_expenditure-Government.total_public_tax_income +Government.bailouts

docs.title: Inflation."inflation_adjustment_b$_per_bc$"
docs.text:
aux

Units: b$/bc$ · Dimensions: Run

“How many nominal dollars correspond to one constant 2021 dollar?”

Inflation Index = the cumulative price level relative to the base year (2021). unit_b$_per_bc$ = a unit-conversion constant (1 nominal B$ per 1 constant bc$ in the base year).

If Inflation Index = 1.30 → inflation adjustment = 1.30 nominal B$ per 1 bc$.

Equation: 

Inflation.Inflation_Index*Inflation."unit_b$_per_bc$"

docs.title: Inflation.energy_supply_growth_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl/year · Dimensions: Run

The equation computes the smoothed annual growth rate of total energy output. It tells you how fast energy supply is increasing or decreasing, averaged over one year.

Using INFLATION_IMPACT_GROWTH_RATE allows FRIDA to capture the trend growth of energy supply, not the noisy point-to-point change.

Equation: 

INFLATION_IMPACT_GROWTH_RATE(energy_supply.Total_Energy_Output, 1, Inflation.initial_energy_supply_growth_rate)

Equation:

docs.title: Private_Insurance.Insurance_Deposits
docs.text:
stock

Units: b$ · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Private_Insurance.insurance_premiums, Private_Insurance.insurance_earnings · Outflows: Private_Insurance.private_insurance_payouts

This represent the accumulated private insurance reserve pool that insurers build up in order to be able to pay claims when climate-related risky defaults occur. The model includes this stock because insurance must have a financial buffer; otherwise, payouts would appear exogenous and not constrained by the insurer’s own balance sheet. This insurance deposit increases with insurance premiums and insurance earnings, and decreases with private insurance payouts. The initial value is set to zero, which means the private insurance mechanism starts without pre-existing reserves and must build capacity endogenously once the policy becomes active

Initial value: 

0

docs.title: Private_Insurance.insurance_premiums
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the inflow to insurance deposit each year. It exists because private insurance must be financed by agents in the economy and coverage cannot be provided for free. The equation says that after policy start year, premiums are calculated as normal investment × premium rate. This means premium collection scales with the amount of normal investment activity in the economy, so a larger investment base generates a larger premium base. Conceptually, the model treats premiums as the price paid for transferring climate-related default risk away from the banking system.

Equation: 

Private_Insurance.insurance_premium_rate*Finance.normal_investment*Government_Regulations.fraction_of_default_covered_by_private_insurance

docs.title: Private_Insurance.insurance_earnings
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

It is the annual return earned on the insurance deposit. It is included because insurers do not only rely on premium inflows. Reserve funds are usually invested in relatively safe assets and generate income. The equation multiplies insurance deposit by private debt safe interest, meaning the larger the reserve base, or the higher the safe interest rate, the more the insurer earns each year. This helps prevent the reserve fund from being depleted too quickly.

Equation: 

Private_Insurance.Insurance_Deposits*Finance.average_private_debt_safe_interest

docs.title: Private_Insurance.private_insurance_payouts
docs.text:
flow

Units: b$/year · Dimensions: Run

it is the annual flow of money paid out by insurers to cover climate-caused risky defaults. This will then decrease insurance deposit. Insurance mechanism only matters if it actually absorbs part of the losses that would otherwise remain on bank balance sheets. The equation means payouts rise automatically when climate-driven defaults rise, but only for the fraction that the insurance policy is designed to cover. So this variable operationalizes the stabilizing role of the private insurance policy in the model.

Equation: 

Finance.total_climate_caused_risky_defaults
* Government_Regulations.fraction_of_default_covered_by_private_insurance

docs.title: Private_Insurance.desired_years_of_coverage
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: years · Dimensions: Run · Range: 1 – 6

It is the target or norm for how many years of coverage insurers would like to maintain. In this model the value is fixed at 4 years, meaning the insurer is assumed to be comfortable if reserves can finance roughly four years of payouts at the current claim rate. It anchors the premium-adjustment mechanism: premiums respond to whether actual coverage is above or below this desired level.

Equation: 

4

docs.title: Private_Insurance.insurance_premium_rate
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It is the effective premium rate applied to the investment base to calculate premium inflows. The model needs a pricing mechanism for insurance that can respond endogenously to risk conditions and reserve adequacy. It is shaped by: (1) normal insurance premium, which is baseline cost under normal climate risk, (2) minimum insurance premium, which is a lower bound so insurance never becomes unrealistically cheap, (3) effect of years of coverage on premiums, where longer coverage leads to higher premium due to uncertainty and climate risk can grow over-time.

Equation: 

MIN(MAX(Private_Insurance.minimum_insurance_premium, Private_Insurance.normal_insurance_premium*Private_Insurance.effect_of_years_of_coverage_on_premiums), 1)

docs.title: Private_Insurance.normal_insurance_premium
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.002 – 0.02

It is the baseline premium rate under normal conditions.Its value is 0.005, meaning the baseline premium is 0.5% of normal investment once the policy is active. This parameter acts as the central pricing anchor from which actual premiums move up or down depending on coverage conditions.

Equation: 

.005

docs.title: Private_Insurance.actual_years_of_coverage
docs.text:
aux

Units: year · Dimensions: Run

It measures how long the current insurance reserve could continue paying claims if payouts remained at their current rate. The equation tells how many years of current payouts can be financed by the current reserve stock. A higher value means the insurer is better capitalized relative to ongoing claims, while a lower value means the reserve could be exhausted quickly. This variable is then used to influence premium pricing, so reserve adequacy feeds back into the cost of insurance.

Equation: 

Private_Insurance.Insurance_Deposits//Private_Insurance.private_insurance_payouts

docs.title: Private_Insurance.effect_of_years_of_coverage_on_premiums
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It translates reserve adequacy into a multiplier on premium pricing. It is to capture the idea that insurers adjust premiums depending on how comfortable or stressed they are about the duration of coverage they can support. The equation means the premium effect depends on the relative coverage ratio raised to a sensitivity exponent. Because this variable is a multiplier, it does not itself set the premium, but it changes the baseline premium upward or downward depending on the insurer’s reserve situation.

Equation: 

MAX(1e-6, Private_Insurance.relative_years_of_coverage)^Private_Insurance.sensitivity_of_premiums_to_years_of_coverage

docs.title: Private_Insurance.sensitivity_of_premiums_to_years_of_coverage
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: -2 – -0.2

It controls how strongly premium pricing reacts to the insurer’s relative coverage horizon. This parameter determines the steepness of the premium response rather than the premium level itself The value is -1, so the premium effect is inversely related to relative years of coverage. That means when relative coverage is low, the effect on premiums rises. When relative coverage is high, the effect falls, which is consistent with the idea that tighter reserves make insurance more expensive.

Equation: 

-1

docs.title: Private_Insurance.relative_years_of_coverage
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

It normalizes the insurer’s actual reserve position against its desired benchmark. It is included because premium setting should usually respond to a relative stress indicator rather than to an absolute number of years alone. A value of 1 means the insurer is exactly at its target coverage horizon. Values above 1 mean the reserve fund can cover claims for longer than the desired benchmark, while values below 1 indicate tighter reserve adequacy.

Equation: 

Private_Insurance.actual_years_of_coverage/Private_Insurance.desired_years_of_coverage

docs.title: Private_Insurance.minimum_insurance_premium
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.001 – 0.002

Is the lower bound for the insurance premium rate. It is included so that insurance never becomes unrealistically cheap, even if reserve conditions would otherwise push the endogenous premium downward. Its value is 0.001, meaning the model imposes a minimum premium of 0.1% of the relevant investment base.

Equation: 

0.001

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Energy module simulates global energy supply and demand, the transition from fossil fuels to renewable and nuclear sources, and the greenhouse-gas emissions that result. Unlike integrated assessment models that solve for a cost-optimal pathway, FRIDA's Energy module simulates the system's evolution from calibrated decision rules, market signals (prices and costs), and policy interventions (taxes, subsidies, regulations). Energy investment is therefore an emergent property of the system structure rather than the output of a forward-looking optimization, and the logic of the energy transition arises from interacting constraints and feedbacks. The module operates in continuous time at global scope, capturing the inertia of capital stocks, delays in investment decisions, and the physical constraints of resource extraction and capacity expansion.

The module's structure can be followed along two strands that meet at the supply–demand imbalance. The first follows the money: how total energy investment is determined, how it is allocated across technologies, and how each technology converts that capital into energy supply. The second is demand, built up from GDP per capita and adjusted by climate-driven heating and cooling. The imbalance between the two strands drives investment, closing the central loop.

docs.title: Energy demand
docs.text:
Energy demand is represented on a per-person basis, irrespective of source. The module tracks how much energy each person demands rather than resolving demand by fuel, carrier, or end-use sector; total demand is simply this per-person demand multiplied by population.

Per-person demand is driven primarily by economic development: rising GDP per person increases the energy each person demands, but with a diminishing marginal effect, so demand grows more slowly than income as economies mature.

Climate shapes demand through the heating and cooling needs of a warming world, expressed as population-weighted degree days. Cooling demand scales with cooling degree days — the population-weighted number of days per year warm enough to require cooling (above roughly 22 °C) — which rise with the surface temperature anomaly and push demand up. Heating demand scales with heating degree days — days cold enough to require heating (below roughly 18 °C) — which fall as the climate warms, pulling demand down. Each index is converted to energy units with an intensity coefficient and the two are combined, so warming simultaneously adds cooling load and removes heating load, with the net effect set by the balance between them.

docs.title: Energy supply
docs.text: Supply is built from modular sub-models spanning fossil (coal, oil, gas), renewable (solar, wind, hydropower, biofuels), and nuclear technologies. Each links capital accumulation, endogenous technological change, and climate-driven infrastructure damage. Capacity expands through investment-driven build flows, depreciates over assumed lifetimes, and is reduced by capital damaged from rising surface temperature, so climate impacts propagate directly into infrastructure integrity.

docs.title: Energy investment
docs.text:
A single Energy Investment sub-model sets both the total volume of investment entering the system and its split across competing technologies, representing the aggregated behavior of global energy investors.

Total volume. The aggregate investment decision is driven by the relative mismatch between supply and demand, normalized by total supply, and is governed by a proportional–integral–derivative (PID) controller whose goal is to invest only what is needed to close the gap. The proportional term reacts to the current mismatch, the integral term to accumulated imbalance, and the derivative term anticipates future imbalances from the rate of change. The three weights are calibrated against observed supply and investment over 1980–2023, so investment responds to current disequilibrium, persistent small imbalances, and rapid shifts alike. Conceptually this mirrors control-theoretic behavior in which agents regulate a perceived system state rather than maximize an objective.

Allocation across technologies. Investment is allocated primarily on each technology's marginal cost of supplying an additional unit of output, computed endogenously within each supply sub-model from production costs, existing capital stocks, learning effects, and technology-specific characteristics. Because pure cost minimization does not reproduce observed diversification, a cost "spread" lets a portion of investment flow to higher-cost options. The spread orders opportunities cheapest-first and apportions capital by a calibrated "width": a width of zero gives fully cost-minimizing behavior, an infinite width spreads investment evenly, and intermediate values favor cheaper options without excluding others. Time-dependent, technology-specific cost adjustment factors capture the monetary equivalent of non-cost preferences and historical policy that are not otherwise represented.

Policy levers. Forward-looking taxes and subsidies can be applied to emissions from individual technologies, to whole technology classes, or to decommissioning rates of extraction capital; all act by modifying the effective marginal costs that drive allocation. The framework also supports structural constraints such as a fossil-fuel moratorium beginning in a chosen year, after which new fossil investment halts and capital is reallocated to remaining options.

docs.title: Renewable energy
docs.text:
Solar, wind, hydropower, and biofuels share a common stock-flow and endogenous cost-learning architecture, each with additional structure.

  • Solar and wind use a multi-vintage conveyor for capital, so capacity is tracked by age cohort to represent aging, productivity, and eventual decommissioning. The reducible installation cost falls with cumulative construction along a power-law learning-by-doing curve. This reinforcing loop is tempered by a "stepping on toes" mechanism in which unit costs rise with the ratio of current to perceived cumulative construction, capturing duplicative effort and bottlenecks during rapid expansion. A floor cost for capacity rises with the solar-and-wind share of total output, representing the growing marginal costs of grid stabilization and storage at high penetration of variable renewables — a balancing feedback on growth. Generation equals accumulated capacity times technology-specific full-load hours. For wind, full-load hours are no longer held constant but respond endogenously to climate change, which mainly acts on the frequency distribution of wind speeds rather than on long-run averages: a rising mean wind speed pushes full-load hours up, while more frequent and longer-lasting low-wind episodes (calms) pull them down, so the net effect on annual wind generation is the balance of these two opposing climate signals.
  • Hydropower is centered on a capacity stock with installation as inflow and two outflows: linear depreciation over an assumed lifetime and climate-driven capital damage. Output is capacity times an efficiency that is itself modulated by climate change (altered river discharge and evaporation). Installation costs follow a linear function that rises with installed capacity, representing the move from prime to marginal sites; the install rate equals investment divided by these capacity-dependent costs, slowing growth as good sites are used up.
  • Biofuels evolve through the same learning and stepping-on-toes cost dynamics, but their production is constrained by the ratio of crop supply to demand, so energy-related crop demand cannot crowd out food. Crops required are derived from available production capital and crop energy density, linking energy to land use. Biofuel primary energy is converted to secondary energy using oil-conversion capacity, integrating it into the liquid-fuel supply chain as a drop-in substitute.

docs.title: Fossil energy
docs.text:
Coal, oil, and gas share an identical structure parameterized per fuel, each split into primary extraction and secondary conversion.

  • Extraction. Extraction capital evolves through gross investment, depreciation, and an explicit decommissioning flow that reflects regulatory constraints or climate damage. Extraction is governed by two opposing forces: resource depletion raises marginal costs as cumulative extraction grows and lower-quality reserves are accessed, while continued extraction drives learning-by-doing that lowers costs. The interaction of geological limits and technological progress determines how fossil competitiveness evolves and whether scarcity accelerates or delays the transition.
  • Conversion. Extracted fuel feeds secondary conversion, represented by a constant-elasticity-of-substitution (CES) production function with fuel input and conversion capital as factors, plus a time-varying total factor productivity term reproducing historical gains in fuel-to-energy efficiency. Conversion capital has no explicit decommissioning term because emissions track extracted (burned) fuel rather than conversion efficiency — though efficiency still changes how much extraction, and thus burning, is needed for a given output.

Abrupt cuts in fossil production, for example from policy shocks, can strand assets in the secondary fossil sectors, so transition risks feed back into investment choices and into loan defaults in FRIDA's economy module.

docs.title: Nuclear energy
docs.text: Nuclear capacity grows through installation and is depleted by physical depreciation and climate-driven capital damage (the latter a catchall with wide uncertainty, including near-zero damage). Output is capacity times efficiency, where efficiency is reduced by warming via the share of thermal plants cooled by rivers: as cooling-water temperatures rise, the thermal cycle's efficiency degrades nonlinearly. Installation costs use the same floor-plus-reducible structure as solar and wind, with learning-by-doing, stepping-on-toes, and optional taxes or subsidies.

docs.title: Efficiency reduction in power plants and climate damages
docs.text: Climate feedbacks enter supply in two ways. First, capacity stocks across all technologies lose capital to climate-driven damage tied to the surface temperature anomaly, shortening effective asset life. Second, the efficiency of thermal plants (fossil and nuclear) and of hydropower declines with warming — thermal through cooling-water constraints, hydropower through changed discharge and evaporation. The energy supply sector is therefore both a driver of climate change and constrained by it.

docs.title: Coupling, prices, and emissions
docs.text:
The module exchanges flows with the rest of FRIDA:

  • Emissions. Combustion of coal, oil, gas, and biofuels produces emissions tracked for four species (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, SO₂), which feed the climate module and drive warming.
  • Economy. Energy investments form part of gross investment in the economy module; the supply–demand imbalance and the marginal net cost of energy feed inflation; fossil-asset stranding affects defaults; and taxes and subsidies move the government budget.
  • Drivers in. Economic development, population, and climatic conditions set baseline demand, while climate conditions also reduce thermal and hydro plant efficiency.

docs.title: Energy units
docs.text: Demand, supply, and capacity are tracked in consistent physical energy units across carriers, with climatic heating and cooling indices converted into the same units via intensity coefficients so that all sources, sinks, and the supply–demand imbalance are directly comparable. Where physical relationships are not fully known, parameters carry calibrated uncertainty ranges constrained by comparison to historical observations, enabling large ensemble runs that reveal the range of plausible century-scale outcomes.

docs.title: energy_investments.adaptive_energy_investments_proportional_factor
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: -50 – 0

The larger the P factor is, the stronger the controller reacts to differences between the set point value and the actual value (0 relative supply demand missmatch). Negative values of P causes compensation of differences, positive values would cause amplification of differences.

Equation: 

-10.0862977296706

docs.title: energy_investments.cost_to_build_per_unit
docs.text:
aux

Units: c$Years/kWh · Dimensions:* Run, EnergySource

Equation: 

IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Coal_fuel_extraction THEN fossil_energy_coal.cost_net_of_taxes_of_extra_energy_output_through_fuel_extraction_per_unit[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Coal_energy_conversion THEN fossil_energy_coal.Cost_net_of_taxes_of_Extra_Energy_Output_through_Capital_per_Unit[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Oil_fuel_extraction THEN fossil_energy_oil.cost_net_of_taxes_of_extra_energy_output_through_fuel_extraction_per_unit[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Oil_energy_conversion THEN fossil_energy_oil.Cost_net_of_taxes_of_Extra_Energy_Output_through_Capital_per_Unit[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Gas_fuel_extraction THEN fossil_energy_gas.cost_net_of_taxes_of_extra_energy_output_through_fuel_extraction_per_unit[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Gas_energy_conversion THEN fossil_energy_gas.Cost_net_of_taxes_of_Extra_Energy_Output_through_Capital_per_Unit[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Solar THEN solar_energy.Cost_of_Extra_Energy_Output_through_Capital_per_Unit_net_of_Tax_and_Subsidies[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Wind THEN wind_energy.Cost_of_Extra_Energy_Output_through_Capital_per_Unit_net_of_Tax_and_Subsidies[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Hydropower THEN hydropower_energy.Cost_net_of_taxes_of_Extra_Energy_Output_through_Capital_per_Unit[Run] 
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Nuclear THEN nuclear_energy.Cost_net_of_taxes_of_Extra_Energy_Output_through_Capital_per_Unit[Run]
ELSE IF EnergySource = EnergySource.Bio THEN bio_fuel_energy."Cost_of_Extra_Energy_Output_through_bio_fuel_production_capacity_per_Unit_net_of_subsidies,_taxes_and_storage_costs"[Run]
ELSE NAN

docs.title: fossil_energy_coal.Secondary_Fossil_Energy_Output
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: TWh/Year · Dimensions: Run

This is total fossil energy production (after burning/transofrmation of fossil fuel)

Calibrate to energy provision from fossil sources

Equation: 

Energy_Units.UNIT_zJ_to_TWh*
(fossil_energy_coal.Total_Factor_Productivity_Fossil_Energy*(
fossil_energy_coal.share_of_primary_energy_in_fossil_energy*
(fossil_energy_coal.Primary_Fossil_Energy_Scaling_Parameter*fossil_energy_coal.Primary_Fossil_Energy_FUEL)^
(fossil_energy_coal.Substitution_Parameter_in_Fossil_Energy_Output)
+
(1-fossil_energy_coal.share_of_primary_energy_in_fossil_energy) *
(fossil_energy_coal.Fossil_Energy_Capital_Scaling_Parameter*fossil_energy_coal.Fossil_Energy_Capital_Orders_of_Mag_Scale*fossil_energy_coal.Fossil_Energy_Capital)^(fossil_energy_coal.Substitution_Parameter_in_Fossil_Energy_Output)
)^(1/fossil_energy_coal.Substitution_Parameter_in_Fossil_Energy_Output))

docs.title: fossil_energy_gas.Secondary_Fossil_Energy_Output
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: TWh/Year · Dimensions: Run

This is total fossil energy production (after burning/transofrmation of fossil fuel)

Calibrate to energy provision from fossil sources

Equation: 

Energy_Units.UNIT_zJ_to_TWh*
(fossil_energy_gas.Total_Factor_Productivity_Fossil_Energy*(
fossil_energy_gas.share_of_primary_energy_in_fossil_energy*
(fossil_energy_gas.Primary_Fossil_Energy_Scaling_Parameter*fossil_energy_gas.Primary_Fossil_Energy_FUEL)^
(fossil_energy_gas.Substitution_Parameter_in_Fossil_Energy_Output)
+
(1-fossil_energy_gas.share_of_primary_energy_in_fossil_energy) *
(fossil_energy_gas.Fossil_Energy_Capital_Scaling_Parameter*fossil_energy_gas.Fossil_Energy_Capital_Orders_of_Mag_Scale*fossil_energy_gas.Fossil_Energy_Capital)^(fossil_energy_gas.Substitution_Parameter_in_Fossil_Energy_Output)
)^(1/fossil_energy_gas.Substitution_Parameter_in_Fossil_Energy_Output))

docs.title: solar_energy.sensitivity_of_renewable_floor_costs_to_grid_storage_needs
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 2 – 10

These parameters are set so that the effect of having to build grid energy storage for renewable output share on floor costs follows a behaviour where costs increase (do not decrease as fast) if grid storage has to be built alongside solar and wind when they make up a large share of output. But if solar and wind is scaled further (including implied complementary storage) costs come down again. The eventual decrease of costs is motivated by the same general idea as overall learning by doing.

Equation: 

5.24737796667638

docs.title: solar_energy.sensitivity_of_renewable_floor_costs_to_grid_storage_needs_non_linear_part
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: -46 – -3

These parameters are set so that the effect of having to build grid energy storage for renewable output share on floor costs follows a behaviour where costs increase (do not decrease as fast) if grid storage has to be built alongside solar and wind when they make up a large share of output. But if solar and wind is scaled further (including implied complementary storage) costs come down again. The eventual decrease of costs is motivated by the same general idea as overall learning by doing.

Equation: 

-5.19750102684353

docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run · Range: 2 – 10

These parameters are set so that the effect of having to build grid energy storage for renewable output share on floor costs follows a behaviour where costs increase (do not decrease as fast) if grid storage has to be built alongside solar and wind when they make up a large share of output. But if solar and wind is scaled further (including implied complementary storage) costs come down again. The eventual decrease of costs is motivated by the same general idea as overall learning by doing.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
The Government Regulations module is where FRIDA's climate and economic policy levers live. It does not generate policy from first principles; instead it holds the exogenous, scenario-defined settings that a user chooses, and it converts those settings into signals that the rest of the model responds to endogenously. In FRIDA's design, the processes that create taxes and subsidies are out of scope, but their effects are fully represented. This means that with one exception, the module never overrides behavior. It changes the incentives, prices, and constraints that the human actors in the model face, and those actors then react through the same feedback structures that govern the baseline.

The module is the natural home for "what-if" policy experiments. A baseline run (the Endogenous Model Behavior scenario) introduces no additional policy, so it behaves like an SSP-style baseline. A mitigation scenario is built by switching on policy structures or changing the levers held here over time.

docs.title: Kinds of policy represented
docs.text:
The module exposes levers across several domains:

  • Energy and carbon pricing. Energy taxes and subsidies, including a carbon tax, which raises the effective cost of carbon-intensive fuels relative to low-carbon alternatives.
  • Support for low-carbon energy and carbon management. Subsidies and public research-and-development support that lower the relative cost of renewables, nuclear, and the carbon capture technologies (end-of-pipe capture, bioenergy with carbon capture, and direct air capture). For carbon management, the levers take the form of desired capture rates and storage shares, plus the maximum share of private investment that may flow to capture and storage.
  • Land-use policy. Control over forestation, irrigation, and non-agricultural water use.
  • Fiscal and macroeconomic settings. Government austerity behavior via a public debt-to-GDP threshold, central-bank inflation and unemployment targets, taxes on profits, wages, and wealth, and sea-level-rise adaptation spending.
  • Demand-side behavior. Levers on food and energy demand and on diet shift. Explicit dietary and energy-demand overrides are the one place where the module directly substitutes for endogenous behavior rather than nudging it.

docs.title: How a carbon price or regulation propagates
docs.text:
A carbon price enters as an addition to the effective cost of fossil-derived energy. In the Energy module, investment in new capacity is allocated across coal, oil, gas, biofuel, hydropower, solar, wind, and nuclear in proportion to their relative marginal costs, with priority given to the least-cost sources. Shifting relative costs through a carbon tax or a subsidy therefore redirects investment toward low-carbon sources, and because the sources that attract more investment can supply more energy, market shares and the fuel mix change over time. Less fossil combustion means lower emissions feeding the Emissions sub-module, and hence a lower trajectory of greenhouse-gas concentrations, radiative forcing, and surface temperature.

The same price signal also propagates through the economy. Higher energy costs raise the average marginal net cost of energy, which feeds the Economy module and can show up as inflation; mitigation scenarios typically show an initial rise in the cost of energy that eases as low-carbon technologies mature through learning-by-doing. On the behavioral side, prices and incomes are inputs to the perceived accessibility of food and energy consumption, so fiscal levers also reshape demand.

Carbon-management policy works through a desired-capture pathway: the levers set desired storage shares and capture fractions, which the Resources/CCS structure turns into desired capacity additions and investment, subject to a budget cap and physical limits. The resulting captured carbon feeds back to the Emissions sub-module, reducing net additions to the atmosphere.

docs.title: Building scenarios and the link to fiscal/monetary policy
docs.text:
Scenarios are specified by setting these levers, often as values that ramp between target years (for example, rising capture or storage shares in 2030, 2050, and 2070). Low, medium, and high variants are commonly produced by scaling a central pathway up or down.

Crucially, policy is not free. Taxes and subsidies affect government budgets, so a subsidy must be offset by adjustments to spending elsewhere to avoid excessive debt. This couples the Government Regulations module directly to the fiscal and monetary machinery in the Economy module, where the government funds spending through taxes and borrowing and cuts expenditure once public debt-to-GDP exceeds its threshold, and where the central bank pursues its inflation and unemployment targets. In this way the policy levers set here are constrained and partly absorbed by the same stock-flow-consistent government and central-bank sectors that drive the rest of the economy.

docs.title: Government_Regulations.forestation_policy
docs.text:
aux Policy lever

Units: Mha/year · Graphical function

To determine the maximum afforestation rate per year, the distribution of all scenarios described in the AR6 report for the variable Land Cover|Forest|Afforestation and Reforestation was used as a basis Based on this distribution, the median values ​​within the 90% quantile range were calculated for each year Since the afforestation value is cumulative over time, the maximum afforestation per year was computed as the average annual rate, using 2010 and 2100 as the starting and ending years, respectively. This calculation resulted in a value of 9 Mha/year. Williams et al. (2024) estimates a potential of 215 million hectares for natural tropical forest regeneration over 30 years, which corresponds to an average annual rate of ~7 Mha/year. This aligns reasonably well with the 9 Mha/year rate derived from the AR6 report, indicating some level of consistency between the findings

Source: https://data.ece.iiasa.ac.at/ar6/#/login?redirect=%2Fworkspaces https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-024-08106-4

Equation: 

TIME

docs.title: How it works
docs.text: The Land Use & Agriculture module is FRIDA's representation of the terrestrial biosphere: how human demand for food, feed, fibre and energy is translated into land use and production, and how the resulting flows of carbon, water and nutrients feed back to the climate, the carbon cycle, and indicators of biodiversity. It follows the structure of dynamic global vegetation models such as LPJmL, but rebuilt as a simplified, fully endogenous, global system-dynamics model with no spatial disaggregation. Carbon enters the land through plant growth, is partly harvested for human use, and the remainder cycles through litter and soil before returning to the atmosphere. Around this carbon-and-water backbone sit nine coupled sub-models, spanning demand (Food Demand, Animal Products), allocation (Land Use), the three production systems (Crop, Grass, Forest), the supporting cycles (Land Nutrients, Freshwater, Soil carbon), and the diagnostic Biosphere Indicators.

docs.title: From demand to land
docs.text:
Demand originates outside the biosphere. Food Demand scales with population and per-capita income from the Demographics and Economy modules and with diet preferences from the Behavioral Change module, splitting into demand for vegetal calories and for animal products. Animal Products converts the animal-product demand into requirements for feed (grown as crops) and for grazing (supplied by grassland), and is the dominant source of agricultural non-CO2 emissions: ruminant digestion and manure produce methane and nitrous oxide.

These demands drive production through two levers: productivity (yield per area) and land area. When supply plus reserves falls short of demand, pressure builds to expand the relevant land system, while intensification raises yields on existing land. Diet therefore propagates strongly through the module — less meat lowers feed-crop demand, intensification pressure, and the associated nutrient and methane flows.

docs.title: Land allocation and land-use change
docs.text:
The Land Use sub-model allocates global habitable land among five stocks — cropland, grassland, young forest, mature forest, and a small degraded-land stock — treated as a compartment model with dynamic transition rates. The young/mature forest split captures the long delay (decades) between planting and a forest reaching its mature carbon density.

Land-use change (LUC) moves area between these stocks in several directions at once:

  • Cropland expands by clearing mature forest and grassland when crop supply lags demand, and is depleted by fallowing to grassland, reforestation, degradation under intensified cropping, and any exogenous government forestation.
  • Grassland expands to maintain its grazing/non-grazing ratio as animal-product demand grows, and is depleted by conversion to cropland, degradation, and (re)forestation.
  • Degraded land accumulates degraded cropland and grassland and is only recovered through forestation policy.
  • Forest receives all reforestation and matures from young to mature stock with a long calibrated delay; mature forest is cut for timber, which returns area to young forest. Total forest is capped at its roughly-1750 extent.

Because production is the product of productivity and area, LUC is the second route to meeting demand; under endogenous behavior, rising food and animal-product demand expands cropland and grassland at the expense of forest over the century, generating large land-use-change emissions.

docs.title: Crop yields and climate impacts
docs.text:
Plant growth in all three systems is built on a generalised multi-linear regression for net primary productivity (NPP), originally fit to gridded crop-model output. Baseline NPP is scaled by the global temperature anomaly and atmospheric CO2: a positive linear CO2-fertilisation term, a positive linear temperature term, and a negative quadratic temperature term that reproduces the non-linear heat-damage seen in temperature-response curves. For cropland, three additional terms add the effects of soil carbon, irrigation, and fertilisation, so intensification raises crop NPP. On Crop and Grass land carbon turns over annually; on Forest it accumulates as aboveground biomass, with young forest allocating more NPP to growth and mature-forest growth tapering toward a temperature-dependent stable density.

The single aggregated crop yield is the harvested fraction of crop NPP, set by a harvest index that depends on end use (lowest for food, higher for feed, highest for whole-plant energy crops) and that rises with productivity to mimic improved crop breeds. The climate impact on yield is calibrated from multi-model crop simulations under systematically perturbed temperature, CO2, water and nitrogen: yield change is linear in CO2 concentration with first- and second-order temperature effects, so warming and CO2 fertilisation pull in opposite directions. The same response also scales bioenergy-crop output. Residues not harvested become litter; high biomass use therefore leaves less litter to build soil carbon.

docs.title: Nutrients and freshwater
docs.text:
Land Nutrients represents fertilisation — the dominant intensification channel alongside irrigation. Total nitrogen and phosphorus application combines synthetic fertiliser with manure recycled from animal husbandry; manure substitution and ruminant numbers tie nutrient flows back to diet. Fertilisation raises crop NPP but the surplus drives the nitrogen and phosphorus loads used as biogeochemical-flow indicators.

Freshwater is a global bookkeeping stock of available water supply, recharged naturally and by technological discovery (deeper wells, reservoirs) and drawn down by withdrawals. Non-agricultural withdrawal scales with population and income; agricultural withdrawal is the irrigated cropland area times a per-area rate that falls with improving irrigation efficiency and rises with warming-driven evapotranspiration. A small unsustainable fraction draws on groundwater, whose decline contributes to sea-level rise and couples to the Climate module; irrigation also returns water vapour and surface-albedo changes as climate forcings.

docs.title: Soil carbon
docs.text: Litter from each land system decomposes into soil organic carbon (SOC), emitting some carbon directly as CO2. Each of cropland, grassland and forest carries its own fast- and slow-decomposing SOC pools with different mean residence times; decay accelerates with temperature, so warming releases stored soil carbon — a positive feedback to the carbon cycle. Because emissions are proportional to stock size and stocks integrate litter input, an unchanging land system tends toward equilibrium. LUC breaks that equilibrium: shifting SOC between systems and reducing carbon input (e.g. forest to cropland) makes the land a CO2 source, while afforestation makes it a sink.

aux

Ref for using GDP per person to determine Crop Demand (Tilman et al. 2011)

Tilman D, Balzer C, Hill J, Befort BL. 2011. Global food demand and the sustainable intensification of agriculture. Proceedings of the national academy of sciences 108(50):20260-20264.

Equation: 

Food_Demand.crop_demand_for_food + Food_Demand.crop_demand_for_feed + Food_Demand.crop_demand_for_energy+
Food_Demand.Crops_used_for_Seed_or_Lost+

docs.title: Food_Demand.direct_food_demand_per_person_per_day
docs.text:
aux

Units: kCal/Person/Day · Dimensions: Run

Equation: 

IF TIME > Behavioral_Choices.start_year AND Behavioral_Choices.switch_food_demand_policy THEN Behavioral_Choices.food_demand_policy
ELSE IF Food_Demand.switch_use_GDP_for_food_demand THEN
Food_Demand.direct_food_demand_per_person_per_day_1980*(Food_Demand.perceived_GDP_per_person/INIT(Food_Demand.perceived_GDP_per_person))^Food_Demand.elasticity_of_food_demand_to_GDP_per_person
ELSE Total_Food_Demand.average_daily_demand_per_capita

docs.title: Forest.minimum_fraction_of_normal_mature_forest_aboveground_biomass_per_area
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl

When STA grows past 7 deg C, how low can mature aboveground forest biomass be? Total estimate of 25% of the "normal" but this is defensible because we know its not 0, and at an STA that high, so much has happened that we're really in uncharted territory for all of the climate feedback functions.

Limits for extreme conditions in no feedback cases

Equation: 

.25

docs.title: Forest.tolerance_for_minimum_fraction_of_normal_mature_forest_aboveground_biomass_per_area
docs.text:

docs.title: Freshwater.available_water_supply
docs.text:
stock Calibrated

Units: m^3 · Dimensions: Run · Inflows: Freshwater.natural_water_recharge_flow, Freshwater.technological_water_discovery · Outflows: Freshwater.total_water_withdrawal

From Billy and Lan: This is water that can be used by humans for ag (irrigation), domestic, industry

This is after water has been reserved for ecological systems. If we want to modify that amount, we would add an outflow, and control that as a policy.

Water is withdrawn by people (for drinking and industry) using GDP per person to model the per person consumption for those purposes then multiplying by Population

Water is withdrawn for irrigation based on a fraction of cropland irrigated (which itself is driven off of relative water availability. Water withdrawn (i.e. lost) on a per unit of crop production basis is driven by technology (down), climate (up).

Irrigation effects crop yield, but does not set it to 0 when the available water supply runs out, because of rain.

The available water supply is recharged at a natural constant rate (safe assumption re: Lan). It can also be supplemented by humans who "drill deeper" via technological water discovery, and the pressure to increase that, as water supplies dwindle.

Initial value: 

Freshwater.initial_available_water_supply

docs.text:
flow

Units: Mha/year · Dimensions: Run

FRIDA allows conversion of Unused Land into Agricultural Land (Edgerton 2009; Lambin and Meyfroidt 2011)

Edgerton MD. 2009. Increasing crop productivity to meet global needs for feed, food, and fuel. Plant Physiol 149(1): 7-13. doi: 10.1104/pp.108.130195.

Lambin EF, Meyfroidt P. 2011. Global land use change, economic globalization, and the looming land scarcity. Proceedings of the national academy of sciences 108(9): 3465-3472. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1100480108.

Equation: 

MIN(Land_Use.Grassland/DT,  Land_Use.new_land_for_crops*(1-Land_Use.fraction_of_new_cropland_from_forests))

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Resources module covers two material-and-emissions systems that sit at the interface between the human economy and the carbon cycle: carbon capture and storage (CCS), and the production and stock of concrete. The concrete sub-model is a fully developed stock-flow system driven by demand, building lifecycle, deterioration, and policy. The CCS sub-model is preliminary and not production ready — an area of active development (see below). Both draw fossil-fuel and bioenergy emissions from the Emissions and Energy modules, take policy levers from the Government Regulations module, and return carbon flows that feed back into the carbon cycle.

docs.title: Carbon capture and storage (preliminary)
docs.text:
Preliminary — not production ready

The CCS sub-model is an early placeholder and an area of active development. It is wired into the rest of FRIDA but its mechanism is deliberately simple, and in the base configuration its policy levers are set to zero, so it captures and stores no carbon. Results should not rely on it until it is further developed.

docs.title: Concrete and process emissions
docs.text:
The Concrete sub-model tracks the global in-use stock of concrete across four stocks: new and old residential-and-service buildings, and new and old infrastructure. Demand for buildings is built from desired useful floor area, a logarithmic function of GDP per person scaled by population and converted to concrete via a concrete-per-floor-area intensity. Infrastructure demand is a regression on population, GDP per capita, and their interaction. In each case, the gap between desired and installed stock drives a construction flow, smoothed over an adjustment time.

Concrete moves through a lifecycle: new structures age into the old stock at a cutpoint of half their average lifetime, old structures are eventually decommissioned, and both deteriorate continuously through wear. New construction and maintenance (replacing deteriorated material) together constitute total yearly concrete use.

Each tonne of concrete produced carries process and material CO₂ emissions, computed from construction-plus-maintenance throughput times a CO₂ intensity specific to building versus infrastructure mixes. These emissions feed the carbon cycle. The model represents the production-side emissions of cement and concrete; it does not credit any carbonation re-uptake as a CO₂ sink — carbonation appears only as a corrosion mechanism that, with chloride exposure, shortens concrete service life.

docs.title: Climate feedbacks and coupling
docs.text: Rising surface temperature shortens concrete service life, accelerating aging, deterioration, and the replacement construction (and its emissions) needed to hold stocks. Extreme-weather storm damage, valued from coastal-asset damages in the Climate module, adds to deterioration of older structures. Concrete production in turn adds process and material CO₂ to the carbon cycle, closing a loop to warming. (CCS would also couple to the carbon cycle, but is inactive in the base configuration — see above.)

docs.title: Concrete.new_RS_construction
docs.text:
flow Calibrated

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the construction of new residential and service buildings measured in million tonnes of concrete per year. It is computed as the gap between existing and desired RS floor area, multiplied by the concrete intensity of floor area and divided by the average adjustment time for closing this gap.

Equation: 

Concrete.gap_in_useful_floor_area*Concrete.CONCRETE_PER_m2_UFA/Concrete.GAP_ADJUSTMENT_TIME_FOR_BUILDINGS

docs.title: Concrete.new_infrastructure_construction
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the construction of new infrastructure measured in million tonnes of concrete per year. It is computed as the gap between existing and desired infrastructure divided by the average adjustment time for closing this gap. The rate is bounded by zero to avoid negative construction (demolition of existing young infrastructures).

Equation: 

MAX(0, 
Concrete.infrastructure_gap/
Concrete.GAP_ADJUSTMENT_TIME_FOR_INFRASTRUCTURE)

docs.title: Concrete.RS_aging
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the rate determining how much of new Residential and Service buildings transition to the stock of old RS buildings each year. The flow is computed as the stock value of New RS buildings divided by the average lifetime of new RS buildings.

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_New_Residential_and_Service_Buildings/(Concrete.AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_BUILDINGS)*Concrete.AGE_CUTPOINT_FOR_CONCRETE_STRUCTURES

docs.title: Concrete.infrastructure_aging
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the rate determining how much of new infrastructure transitions to the stock of old infrastructure each year. The flow is computed as the stock value of New infrastructure divided by the average lifetime of new infrastructure.

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_new_Infrastructure/(Concrete.AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_INFRASTRUCTURE)*
Concrete.AGE_CUTPOINT_FOR_CONCRETE_STRUCTURES

docs.title: Concrete.decommissioning_RS_buildings
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

Old residential and service buildings are decommissioned at a rate determined by their average lifetime.

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_Old_Residential_and_Service_Buildings/
(Concrete.AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_BUILDINGS)*Concrete.AGE_CUTPOINT_FOR_CONCRETE_STRUCTURES

docs.title: Concrete.decommissioning_infrastructure
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

Old infrastructure is decommissioned at a rate determined by their average lifetime.

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_old_Infrastructure/
(Concrete.AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_INFRASTRUCTURE)*Concrete.AGE_CUTPOINT_FOR_CONCRETE_STRUCTURES

docs.title: Concrete.Old_RS_maintenance
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the flow of concrete required to replace deterioration losses due to wear and tear to maintain the stock level. The flow is computed as the deterioration rate multiplied by the maintenance efficiency.

Equation: 

Concrete.Old_RS_deterioration*
Concrete.RS_MAINTENANCE_EFFICIENCY

docs.title: Concrete.old_infrastructure_maintenance
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the flow of concrete required to replace deterioration losses due to wear and tear to maintain the stock level. The flow is computed as the deterioration rate multiplied by the maintenance efficiency.

Equation: 

Concrete.old_infrastructure_deterioration*
Concrete.infrastructure_maintenance_efficiency

docs.title: Concrete.Old_RS_deterioration
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This flow of concrete represents the deterioration of old residential and service buildings due to wear and tear plus additional damages due to extreme weather events.

The flow is computed as the existing stock multiplied by the NORMAL CONCRETE DETERIORATION RATE parameter plus storm damages to service and residential buildings. The Storm damages are computed as the total storm damages to concrete buildings and infrastructure multiplied by the share of old concrete in the system that is used in residential and service buildings.

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_Old_Residential_and_Service_Buildings*
Concrete.NORMAL_CONCRETE_DETERIORATION_RATE

+

Concrete.storm_damages_to_concrete_buildings_and_infrastructure * (1-Concrete.share_of_old_concrete_in_infrastructure)

docs.title: Concrete.old_infrastructure_deterioration
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This flow of concrete represents the deterioration of old infrastructure due to wear and tear plus additional damages due to extreme weather events.

The flow is computed as the existing stock multiplied by the NORMAL CONCRETE DETERIORATION RATE parameter plus storm damages to infrastructure. The Storm damages to infrastructure is computed as the total storm damages to concrete buildings and infrastructure multiplied by the share of old concrete in the system that is used in infrastructure.

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_old_Infrastructure*
Concrete.NORMAL_CONCRETE_DETERIORATION_RATE

+

Concrete.storm_damages_to_concrete_buildings_and_infrastructure*Concrete.share_of_old_concrete_in_infrastructure

docs.title: Concrete.New_RS_deterioration
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This flow of concrete represents the deterioration of new residential and service buildings due to wear and tear. The flow is computed as the existing stock multiplied by the NORMAL CONCRETE DETERIORATION RATE parameter.

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_New_Residential_and_Service_Buildings*
Concrete.NORMAL_CONCRETE_DETERIORATION_RATE

docs.title: Concrete.new_infrastructure_deterioration
docs.text:
flow

Concrete.NORMAL_CONCRETE_DETERIORATION_RATE

docs.title: Concrete.New_RS_maintenance
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the flow of concrete required to replace deterioration losses due to wear and tear to maintain the stock level. Perfect maintenance is assumed for new structures.

Concrete.New_RS_deterioration

docs.title: Concrete.new_infrastructure_maintenance
docs.text:
flow

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the flow of concrete required to replace deterioration losses due to wear and tear to maintain the stock level. Perfect maintenance is assumed for new structures.

Equation: 

Concrete.new_infrastructure_deterioration

docs.title: Concrete.desired_useful_floor_area
docs.text:
aux

Units: Mm2 · Dimensions: Run

This is the delayed desired floor area in service and residential buildings, accounting for the time it takes for demand to adjust to changes in wealth or population size.

Equation: 

SMTH3(Concrete.indicated_desired_per_capita_floor_area[Run] * Demographics.Population[Run], 
Concrete.TIME_TO_ADJUST_DESIRED_FLOOR_AREA[Run])

docs.title: Concrete.actual_useful_floor_area
docs.text:
aux

Units: Mm2 · Dimensions: Run

This is the total useful floor area in residential and service buildings.

Equation: 

(Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_Old_Residential_and_Service_Buildings+Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_New_Residential_and_Service_Buildings)/Concrete.CONCRETE_PER_m2_UFA

docs.title: Concrete.gap_in_useful_floor_area
docs.text:
aux

Units: Mm2 · Dimensions: Run

This is the difference between the desired floor area and the existing floor area. The gap is bounded by zero as we assume there is never a demand to reduce useful floor area.

Equation: 

MAX(0, Concrete.desired_useful_floor_area-Concrete.actual_useful_floor_area)

docs.title: Concrete.GAP_ADJUSTMENT_TIME_FOR_BUILDINGS
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.5 – 20

This is the time delay for construction of new buildings in response to a floor area gap.

Calibration Range: 0.5-20 years Source: Assumption

Equation: 

[1] = 13.9260948345179

docs.title: Concrete.TIME_TO_ADJUST_DESIRED_FLOOR_AREA
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: Years · Dimensions: Run · Range: 2 – 30

This is the time delay for floor area demand to adjust to changes in wealth. Calibration range: 2 - 30 years Source: Assumption

Equation: 

[1] = 6.33255854211754

docs.title: Concrete.CONCRETE_PER_m2_UFA
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: Mtonne/Mm2 · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.452 – 1.43

This is the mean concrete content per m^2 UFA based on data from Marinova et al (2020). The interquartile range for the same data set is 452 - 1430 kg/m^2.

Source: DOI: 10.1016/j.jclepro.2019.119146

Equation: 

[1] = 0.998

docs.title: Concrete.indicated_desired_per_capita_floor_area
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: Mm2/Mp · Dimensions: Run

The effect of wealth (GDP per cap) on desired UFA is based on data on average UFA and GDP from European countries.

The functional form of the relationship was derived by regressing UFA/cap on GDP/cap. Parameter were then calibrated to match historical concrete use in Residential and Service buildings.

UFA per capita data: https://entranze.enerdata.net/average-floor-area-per-capita.html

GDP per capita data: https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/gdp-per-capita-maddison?time=2008®ion=Europe

Equation: 

MAX(
0, 
Concrete.UFA_alpha*LN(Demographics.real_GDP_per_person*1000) + Concrete.UFA_intercept)

docs.title: Concrete.AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_BUILDINGS
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: Year · Dimensions: Run

This is the average lifetime of residential and service buildings when accounting for longevity policies in place and the effect of climate change (STA) on concrete durability.

Equation: 

Concrete.BASE_AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_BUILDINGS *
Concrete.actual_concrete_residential_longevity_policy *
Concrete.effect_of_STA_on_average_lifetime_of_concrete

docs.title: Concrete.residential_concrete_stock_in_use
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: Mtonne · Dimensions: Run

Global construction materials database and stock analysis of residential buildings between 1970-2050

S. Marinova, S. Deetman, E. van der Voet and V. Daioglou

Journal of Cleaner Production 2020 Vol. 247

DOI: 10.1016/j.jclepro.2019.119146

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_New_Residential_and_Service_Buildings + Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_Old_Residential_and_Service_Buildings

This is the mass of concrete in the Old buildings stock at the start of the simulation (1980).

docs.title: Concrete.total_yearly_concrete_use
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

U.S. Geological Survey, 2019, Cement statistics, in Kelly, T.D., and Matos, G.R., comps., Historical statistics for mineral and material commodities in the United States: U.S. Geological Survey Data Series 140, accessed [date], at:

https://www.usgs.gov/centers/nmic/historical-statistics-mineral-and-material-commodities-united-states.

Unit: million metric tonne/year

Assumption: Available data is in cement production. To estimate global concrete production we assume a concrete mix consisting of 15% cement by mass.

Equation: 

Concrete.New_RS_maintenance + Concrete.Old_RS_maintenance + Concrete.new_infrastructure_maintenance + Concrete.old_infrastructure_maintenance + Concrete.new_infrastructure_construction + Concrete.new_RS_construction

docs.title: Concrete.In_use_concrete_stock
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: Mtonne · Dimensions: Run

Global socioeconomic material stocks rise 23-fold over the 20th century and require half of annual resource use

F. Krausmann, D. Wiedenhofer, C. Lauk, W. Haas, H. Tanikawa, T. Fishman, et al.

Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 2017 Vol. 114 Issue 8 Pages 1880-1885

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5338421/pdf/pnas.201613773.pdf

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_New_Residential_and_Service_Buildings + Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_Old_Residential_and_Service_Buildings + Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_new_Infrastructure + Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_old_Infrastructure

docs.title: Concrete.concrete_waste_generation
docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

This is the sum of decommissioned and deteriorated concrete.

Equation: 

Concrete.New_RS_deterioration + Concrete.new_infrastructure_deterioration + Concrete.Old_RS_deterioration + Concrete.decommissioning_RS_buildings + Concrete.decommissioning_infrastructure + Concrete.old_infrastructure_deterioration

docs.text:
aux

This is the total installed stock of infrastructure measured in million tons of concrete.

(Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_old_Infrastructure+Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_new_Infrastructure)

docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

This represents the time it takes for the demand for infrastructure to update when population and/or wealth changes.

Calibration range: 1 - 10 years Source: Assumption

docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

This is the time delay for construction of new infrastructure in response to a infrastructure gap.

docs.title: Concrete.AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_INFRASTRUCTURE
docs.text:
aux

Units: year · Dimensions: Run

Estimated service life of infrastructure ranges from 25-100 years depending on country and infrastructure type (Global infrastructure Outlook 2017, pp 204).

Link: https://cdn.gihub.org/outlook/live/methodology/Global+Infrastructure+Outlook+-+July+2017.pdf

Equation: 

Concrete.BASE_AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_INFRASTRUCTURE *
Concrete.actual_concrete_infrastructure_longevity_policy *
Concrete.effect_of_STA_on_average_lifetime_of_concrete

docs.text:
aux Calibrated

Units: Mtonne · Dimensions: Run

Calibration data calculated as the difference between data on 'In use concrete stock' (Krausmann et al, 2017) and 'residential concrete stock in use' (Marinova et al, 2020).

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_new_Infrastructure + Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_old_Infrastructure

docs.title: Concrete.RS_MAINTENANCE_EFFICIENCY
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl

This variable computes the actual maintenance efficiency of residential and service buildings by multiplying the NORMAL RS MAINTENANCE EFFICIENCY by the concrete infrastructure maintenance policy variable for residential and service buildings. This allows model users to explore scenarios where maintenance is less ambitious.

Equation: 

Concrete.NORMAL_RS_MAINTENANCE_EFFICIENCY *
Concrete.actual_maintenance_policy

docs.title: Concrete.infrastructure_maintenance_efficiency
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This variable computes the actual infrastructure maintenance efficiency by multiplying the NORMAL INFRASTRUCTURE MAINTENANCE EFFICIENCY by the concrete infrastructure maintenance policy variable. This allows model users to explore scenarios where maintenance is less ambitious.

Equation: 

Concrete.NORMAL_INFRASTRUCTURE_MAINTENANCE_EFFICIENCY *
Government_Regulations.concrete_infrastructure_maintenance_policy

docs.text:

This is the concrete deterioration base rate without climate damages.

Calibration Range: 0.001 - 0.1 Source: Assumption qualitatively based on Krausmann et al (2017) (https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1613773114)

Equation: 

[1] = 0.0142483549785972

aux Assumption

This parameter determines the efficiency of infrastructure maintenance. If set to 1.0, this means perfect maintenance.

Equation: 

1

docs.title: Concrete.NORMAL_RS_MAINTENANCE_EFFICIENCY
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: dmnl

This parameter determines the efficiency of residential and service buildings maintenance. If set to 1.0, this means perfect maintenance.

1

docs.title: Concrete.CO2_emissions_from_concrete_for_HS
docs.text:
aux

Units: MtCO2/Year · Dimensions: Run

This is the CO2 emissions from concrete production for residential and service buildings. It is computed as the sum of concrete used for new constructions and maintenance multiplied by the CO2 intensity of the concrete mix used for these structures.

Equation: 

Concrete.CO2_INTENSITY_OF_RS_BUILDINGS_CONCRETE* 
(Concrete.new_RS_construction+
Concrete.New_RS_maintenance+
Concrete.Old_RS_maintenance)

docs.title: Concrete.CO2_emissions_from_concrete_for_Infrastructure
docs.text:
aux

Units: MtCO2/Year · Dimensions: Run

This is the CO2 emissions from concrete production for infrastructure purposes. It is computed as the sum of concrete used for new constructions and maintenance multiplied by the CO2 intensity of the concrete mix used for these structures.

Equation: 

Concrete.CO2_INTENSITY_OF_INFRASTRUCTURE_CONCRETE*
(Concrete.new_infrastructure_construction+
Concrete.new_infrastructure_maintenance+
Concrete.old_infrastructure_maintenance)

docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: MtCO2/Mtonne · Dimensions: Run · Range: 0.05 – 0.163

Based on reference values for concrete mixes for infrastructure and other non-residential buildings. Mean: 0.104 MtonneCO2/Mtonne concrete Range: 0.087-0.163 MtonCO2/Mtonne concrete

Source: https://www.svenskbetong.se/component/edocman/?task=document.viewdoc&id=104&Itemid=

Billy: this range must be high based on new emissions data from Chris Wells

Equation: 

[1] = 0.0585221156311479

docs.text:

Based on reference values for concrete mixes for infrastructure and other non-residential buildings. Mean: 0.145 MtonneCO2/Mtonne concrete Range: 0.109-0.164 MtonCO2/Mtonne concrete

Source: https://www.svenskbetong.se/component/edocman/?task=document.viewdoc&id=104&Itemid=

Billy: this range must be high based on new emissions data from Chris Wells
