docs.title: Concrete.CO2_emissions
docs.text:
aux

Units: MtCO2/Year · Dimensions: Run

This is the sum of CO2 emissions from concrete production for buildings and infrastructure development.

Equation: 

Concrete.CO2_emissions_from_concrete_for_HS+
Concrete.CO2_emissions_from_concrete_for_Infrastructure

docs.title: Concrete.desired_infrastructure
docs.text:
aux

Units: Mtonne · Dimensions: Run

This is the delayed desired infrastructure demand, accounting for the time it takes for demand to adjust to changes in wealth or population size.

Equation: 

SMTH3(Concrete.indicated_desired_infrastructure, 
Concrete.TIME_TO_ADJUST_DESIRED_INFRASTRUCTURE_AREA)

docs.title: Concrete.infrastructure_gap
docs.text:
aux

Units: Mtonne · Dimensions: Run

This is the difference between the desired infrastructure and the existing infrastructure. The gap is bounded by zero as we assume there is never a demand to infrastructure.

Equation: 

MAX(0, Concrete.desired_infrastructure - Concrete.actual_useful_infrastructure)

docs.title: Concrete.indicated_desired_infrastructure
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: Mtonne · Dimensions: Run

This is the indicated infrastructure demand based on population size and wealth (GDP per capita).

The combined effect of wealth (GDP per cap) and population size on desired infrastructure is based on data on the calibration data used for 'Infrastructure concrete stock in use'.

The functional form of the relationship was derived by regressing Infrastructure on Population, GDP per capita and including the interaction effects between GDP per capita and Population size. Parameter were machine calibrated to match historical concrete use in the rest of the module.

Equation: 

MAX(0, 
Concrete.INTERCEPT + Concrete.INFRA_ALPHA*Demographics.real_GDP_per_person +  Concrete.INFRA_BETA*Demographics.Population + Concrete.INFRA_GAMMA*
Demographics.real_GDP_per_person*Demographics.Population)

docs.title: Concrete.INFRA_ALPHA
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: Mtonne/(bc$/Mp/year) · Dimensions: Run · Range: -40000 – -5000

This is the regression coefficient determining the effect of wealth (GDP per capita) on infrastructure demand.

Calibration Range: -40000 - -5000 Source: Assumption

Equation: 

[1] = -27235.0941605342{- 28801}

docs.title: Concrete.INFRA_BETA
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: Mtonne/Mp · Dimensions: Run · Range: -40 – -5

This is the regression coefficient determining the effect of population size on infrastructure demand.

Calibration Range: -40 - -5 Source: Assumption

Equation: 

[1] = -37.3680135607164{-43.14}

docs.title: Concrete.INFRA_GAMMA
docs.text:
aux Assumption Internal calibration parameter

Units: Mtonne/(bc$/year) · Dimensions: Run · Range: 4.48 – 6.824

This is the regression coefficient determining the interaction effect of wealth (GDP per capita) and populations on infrastructure demand.

Calibration Range: 0.5 - 10 Source: Assumption

Equation: 

[1] = 6.61569213989495
{+5.652}

docs.title: Concrete.CLIMATE_IMPACT_ON_CONCRETE_LIFETIME
docs.text:
aux Climate-impact parameter

Units: dmnl/deg C · Dimensions: Run · Range: -0.15 – -0.05

Climate change impacts the durability and lifetime of concrete structures through carbonation-induced corrosion and chloride-induced corrosion, driven by multiple temperature, CO2 and chloride exposure mechanisms.

Global scale effects are hard to estimate, therefore a ball-park estimate of STA effect on reinforced concrete structure service life where 1 C increase ~ 5-15% reduction in service lifetime has been used.

This qualitative estimate is derived from:

Bastidas-Arteaga, E., & Stewart, M. G.: Damage risks and economic assessment of climate adaptation strategies for design of new concrete structures subject to chloride-induced corrosion, Structural Safety, 52, 40-53, 2015. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.strusafe.2014.10.005

Stewart, M. G., Wang, X., and Nguyen, M-N.,.: Climate change impact and risks of concrete infrastructure deterioration, Engineering Structures, 33(4), 1326-1337. 2011. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.engstruct.2011.01.010

Wang, X., Stewart, M.G., and Nguyen, M,: Impact of climate change on corrosion and damage to concrete infrastructure in Australia, Climatic Change, 110,3, 941-957, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10584-011-0124-7

Equation: 

[1] = -0.075

docs.title: Concrete.BASE_AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_INFRASTRUCTURE
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 25 – 150

This is the average lifetime of concrete infrastructure without climate impacts,

Calibration range: 25-150 years Based on: Common ranges from the literature. E.g., Wang et al. 2012 (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0124-7) and UCSUSA (https://www.ucsusa.org/sites/default/files/attach/gw-smart-infrastructure-table-life-expectancy.pdf).

Equation: 

[1] = 25

docs.title: Concrete.BASE_AVERAGE_LIFETIME_OF_BUILDINGS
docs.text:
aux Internal calibration parameter

Units: year · Dimensions: Run · Range: 75 – 200

This is the average lifetime of concrete buildings without climate impacts,

Calibration range: 75-200 years Based on: Expert opinion. Katarina Malaga (https://www.ri.se/en/person/katarina-malaga) and cross checked with literature (Wang et al. 2012, https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-011-0124-7).

Equation: 

[1] = 115.309006352437

docs.title: Concrete.storm_damages_to_concrete_buildings_and_infrastructure
docs.text:
aux Assumption

Units: Mtonne/year · Dimensions: Run

Storm damages to concrete structures are estimated taking the monetary damages to Coastal Assets computed in the Climate module (measured in billion $ per year) and dividing this value with the computed average value of concrete from the calibration period, 1980-2020, (measured in billion $ per million tonnes of concrete).

Equation: 

IF (Climate.switch_concrete_impact = 0) AND TIME > Climate.climate_impact_stoptime THEN
PREVIOUS(SELF, 0)
ELSE
SUM(Coastal_Assets.Asset_storm_damage_because_of_SLR[Run,*])/Concrete."UNIT_bc$/Mtonne_concrete"

docs.title: Concrete."UNIT_bc$/Mtonne_concrete"
docs.text:
aux Unit helper

Units: bc$/Mtonne

This is the mean historic value (1980-2020) per Mtonne of concrete measured in constant asset dollars.

Asset dollars = Bank Assets minus Government debt.

Equation: 

1.62

docs.title: Concrete.effect_of_STA_on_average_lifetime_of_concrete
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This variable computes the effect of changes in global mean surface temperature (STA) on the longevity of concrete structures.

Equation: 

IF (Climate.switch_concrete_impact = 0) AND TIME > Climate.climate_impact_stoptime THEN
PREVIOUS(SELF, 1)
ELSE
1+Concrete.CLIMATE_IMPACT_ON_CONCRETE_LIFETIME*
(Energy_Balance_Model.Absolute_Surface_Temperature - INIT(Energy_Balance_Model.Absolute_Surface_Temperature))

docs.title: Concrete.share_of_old_concrete_in_infrastructure
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl · Dimensions: Run

This is the share of concrete in aged old structures that sits in the old infrastructure stock. This is used to estimate the proportion of climate damages from extreme weather events that is attributed to infrastructure vs. buildings.

Equation: 

Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_old_Infrastructure/(Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_old_Infrastructure+Concrete.Concrete_Stock_in_Old_Residential_and_Service_Buildings)

docs.title: Concrete.actual_concrete_infrastructure_longevity_policy
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl

This is the policy in place for regulating building standards influencing longevity of concrete infrastructure.

Equation: 

IF TIME < Government_Regulations.start_year THEN 1
ELSE
Government_Regulations.Infrastructure_buildings_longevity_policy

docs.title: Concrete.actual_maintenance_policy
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl

This is the policy in place for regulating building standards influencing longevity of concrete buildings.

Equation: 

IF TIME < Government_Regulations.start_year THEN 1
ELSE
Government_Regulations.concrete_residential_and_service_maintenance_policy

docs.title: Concrete.actual_concrete_residential_longevity_policy
docs.text:
aux

Units: dmnl

This is the policy in place for regulating building standards influencing longevity of concrete buildings.

Equation: 

IF TIME < Government_Regulations.start_year THEN 1
ELSE
Government_Regulations.Residential_and_Service_buildings_longevity_policy

This is the intercept parameter for the regression function used for estimating infrastructure demand as a function of population size and wealth.

Calibration Range: 5000 - 20000 Source: Assumption

{+ 243696}

docs.title: Concrete.AGE_CUTPOINT_FOR_CONCRETE_STRUCTURES
docs.text:
aux Assumption

This is parameter determines when a building is considered new vs. old. Structure are considered old when they have passed half of their estimated average lifetime.

0.5
